abstract: Despite the rapid growth of machine learning research, corresponding code implementations are often unavailable, making it slow and labor-intensive for researchers to reproduce results and build upon prior work. In the meantime, recent Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at understanding scientific documents and generating high-quality code. Inspired by this, we introduce PaperCoder, a multi-agent LLM framework that transforms machine learning papers into operational code repositories. PaperCoder operates in three stages: planning, where it constructs a high-level roadmap, designs the system architecture with diagrams, identifies file dependencies, and generates configuration files; analysis, which focuses on interpreting implementation-specific details; and generation, where modular, dependency-aware code is produced. Moreover, each phase is instantiated through a set of specialized agents designed to collaborate effectively across the pipeline. We then evaluate PaperCoder on generating code implementations from machine learning papers based on both model-based and human evaluations, particularly from the authors of those papers, with author-released repositories as ground truth if available. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of PaperCoder in creating high-quality, faithful implementations. Furthermore, it consistently shows strengths in the recently released PaperBench benchmark, surpassing strong baselines by substantial margins. Code is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: A structured, multi-stage, multi-agent LLM architecture is effective for interpreting extensive, unstructured scientific documents and translating them into functional, repository-level code.

Explicit planning for architectural and logical design, including inter-module dependencies and execution order, is crucial for generating coherent and complete codebases from high-level paper descriptions.
Integrating self-refinement steps within the planning and analysis phases significantly improves the quality of their outputs and reduces downstream errors in code generation.
originalProblem: A significant majority (80.5%) of machine learning papers at top-tier conferences do not provide code implementations, hindering reproducibility and scientific progress.
Researchers spend considerable time and effort manually reverse-engineering methods from papers, slowing down validation and iteration.
Existing LLM-powered scientific tools often rely on pre-existing code or well-defined APIs, limiting their ability to address the pervasive issue of absent code in publications.
results: PaperCoder achieved average scores of 3.68-3.83 in reference-based evaluation and 4.73-4.77 in reference-free evaluation on the new Paper2CodeBench, outperforming all baselines by a notable margin.
The framework obtained the highest replication scores (45.14% with o3-mini-high, 51.14% with Claude 3.5 Sonnet) on the PaperBench Code-Dev benchmark, surpassing dedicated baselines.

Manual execution evaluations revealed that generated code was near-executable, requiring minimal human intervention (average of 0.81% line modifications) for successful execution, and was preferred by original paper authors for completeness and faithfulness.
solution: PaperCoder employs a multi-agent LLM framework that simulates a human software development workflow, breaking down the complex paper-to-code task into three coordinated stages: Planning, Analysis, and Generation (Coding).

The Planning stage converts unstructured paper content into structured implementation abstractions, including overall plan, architecture design (file list, class/sequence diagrams), logic design (execution order, dependencies), and configuration files.

The Analysis stage then generates detailed, file-specific implementation specifications (functional goals, I/O, algorithmic details), which are subsequently used by the Coding stage to sequentially produce the complete code repository, ensuring consistency and completeness.
paper_summary.summary: PaperCoder introduces a multi-agent LLM framework that automatically generates executable, repository-level code directly from scientific machine learning papers. The framework significantly enhances research reproducibility by achieving superior performance on both newly established and existing benchmarks for code generation from text.

title: Paper2Code: Automating Code Generation from Scientific Papers in Machine Learning

abstract:
AI is increasingly playing a pivotal role in transforming how scientific
discoveries are made. We introduce The AI Scientist-v2, an end-to-end agentic
system capable of producing the first entirely AI generated
peer-review-accepted workshop paper. This system iteratively formulates
scientific hypotheses, designs and executes experiments, analyzes and
visualizes data, and autonomously authors scientific manuscripts. Compared to
its predecessor (v1, Lu et al., 2024 arXiv:2408.06292), The AI Scientist-v2
eliminates the reliance on human-authored code templates, generalizes
effectively across diverse machine learning domains, and leverages a novel
progressive agentic tree-search methodology managed by a dedicated experiment
manager agent. Additionally, we enhance the AI reviewer component by
integrating a Vision-Language Model (VLM) feedback loop for iterative
refinement of content and aesthetics of the figures. We evaluated The AI
Scientist-v2 by submitting three fully autonomous manuscripts to a
peer-reviewed ICLR workshop. Notably, one manuscript achieved high enough
scores to exceed the average human acceptance threshold, marking the first
instance of a fully AI-generated paper successfully navigating a peer review.
This accomplishment highlights the growing capability of AI in conducting all
aspects of scientific research. We anticipate that further advancements in
autonomous scientific discovery technologies will profoundly impact human
knowledge generation, enabling unprecedented scalability in research
productivity and significantly accelerating scientific breakthroughs, greatly
benefiting society at large. We have open-sourced the code at
this https URL to foster the future development of
this transformative technology. We also discuss the role of AI in science,
including AI safety.

keyInsights: Successful peer review demonstrates feasibility of fully autonomous scientific research
Tree search enables more systematic experimentation through specialized nodes for different research tasks
Vision-language models can effectively evaluate and improve research artifacts
originalProblem: Previous AI scientist systems relied heavily on human-authored code templates, limiting autonomy
Existing approaches lacked systematic exploration of hypotheses and produced shallow experiments
results: One of three submitted papers accepted at ICLR workshop
System generates complete manuscripts including literature review, experiments, and analysis
Identified limitations in hypothesis novelty and consistency of publication quality
Successfully eliminated dependence on human code templates while maintaining research rigor
solution: Developed an end-to-end system combining idea generation, experiment management, and manuscript writing
Implemented agentic tree search for deeper hypothesis exploration with parallel code generation and debugging
Integrated VLM feedback for improving figure and manuscript quality
paper_summary.summary: An autonomous AI system conducts end-to-end scientific research through agentic tree search, successfully generating a peer-reviewed workshop paper without relying on human-authored code templates, marking the first instance of a fully AI-generated paper passing peer review with a 6.33/10 reviewer score.

title: The AI Scientist-v2: Workshop-Level Automated Scientific Discovery via  Agentic Tree Search

abstract:
Historically, scientific discovery has been a lengthy and costly process,
demanding substantial time and resources from initial conception to final
results. To accelerate scientific discovery, reduce research costs, and improve
research quality, we introduce Agent Laboratory, an autonomous LLM-based
framework capable of completing the entire research process. This framework
accepts a human-provided research idea and progresses through three
stages--literature review, experimentation, and report writing to produce
comprehensive research outputs, including a code repository and a research
report, while enabling users to provide feedback and guidance at each stage. We
deploy Agent Laboratory with various state-of-the-art LLMs and invite multiple
researchers to assess its quality by participating in a survey, providing human
feedback to guide the research process, and then evaluate the final paper. We
found that: (1) Agent Laboratory driven by o1-preview generates the best
research outcomes; (2) The generated machine learning code is able to achieve
state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods; (3) Human
involvement, providing feedback at each stage, significantly improves the
overall quality of research; (4) Agent Laboratory significantly reduces
research expenses, achieving an 84% decrease compared to previous autonomous
research methods. We hope Agent Laboratory enables researchers to allocate more
effort toward creative ideation rather than low-level coding and writing,
ultimately accelerating scientific discovery.

keyInsights: Human-AI collaboration, particularly in a "co-pilot" model, is effective for improving the quality of AI-generated research outputs.
Automated evaluation of research quality often overestimates results compared to human assessment, underscoring the need for human validation.
The choice of LLM backend significantly impacts research outcomes, with `o1-preview` and `o1-mini` performing better for specific tasks than `gpt-4o`.
originalProblem: Scientific research is a lengthy, resource-intensive process that limits the number of ideas researchers can explore simultaneously.
Researchers are often burdened by repetitive, low-level tasks, hindering their focus on creative ideation and high-level experiment design.
The high cost and time investment in conducting research create a bottleneck in accelerating scientific discovery.
results: Reduced research costs by 84%, with a full paper generation costing as little as $2.33 using `gpt-4o`, which also completed the workflow in approximately 19.4 minutes.
The `mle-solver` component achieved state-of-the-art performance on MLE-Bench Kaggle challenges, earning more medals than competitors and surpassing median human performance on 60% of benchmarks.
Human intervention in "co-pilot mode" notably improved the overall quality of research outputs, with papers rated higher (4.38/10) compared to autonomously generated papers (3.8/10).
solution: An LLM-based multi-agent framework orchestrates the entire research process through distinct sequential phases: Literature Review, Experimentation, and Report Writing.
Specialized agents (e.g., PhD, ML Engineer) collaborate within the pipeline, utilizing external tools and iterative refinement loops powered by modules like `mle-solver` and `paper-solver`.
The framework operates in both autonomous mode and a human-guided "co-pilot" mode, allowing researchers to intervene at checkpoints and provide feedback to refine agent performance.
paper_summary.summary: Agent Laboratory, an LLM-based framework from AMD and academic partners, automates the scientific research process, notably reducing research costs to $2.33 per paper and enabling state-of-the-art performance in automated machine learning problem-solving. Human involvement in its co-pilot mode improves the overall quality of research outputs.

title: Agent Laboratory: Using LLM Agents as Research Assistants

abstract: One of the grand challenges of artificial general intelligence is developing agents capable of conducting scientific research and discovering new knowledge. While frontier models have already been used as aides to human scientists, e.g. for brainstorming ideas, writing code, or prediction tasks, they still conduct only a small part of the scientific process. This paper presents the first comprehensive framework for fully automatic scientific discovery, enabling frontier large language models to perform research independently and communicate their findings. We introduce The AI Scientist, which generates novel research ideas, writes code, executes experiments, visualizes results, describes its findings by writing a full scientific paper, and then runs a simulated review process for evaluation. In principle, this process can be repeated to iteratively develop ideas in an open-ended fashion, acting like the human scientific community. We demonstrate its versatility by applying it to three distinct subfields of machine learning: diffusion modeling, transformer-based language modeling, and learning dynamics. Each idea is implemented and developed into a full paper at a cost of less than $15 per paper. To evaluate the generated papers, we design and validate an automated reviewer, which we show achieves near-human performance in evaluating paper scores. The AI Scientist can produce papers that exceed the acceptance threshold at a top machine learning conference as judged by our automated reviewer. This approach signifies the beginning of a new era in scientific discovery in machine learning: bringing the transformative benefits of AI agents to the entire research process of AI itself, and taking us closer to a world where endless affordable creativity and innovation can be unleashed on the world's most challenging problems. Our code is open-sourced at this https URL

keyInsights: AI agents can autonomously perform the entire scientific research lifecycle, from ideation and experimentation to paper writing and simulated peer review.
Integrating LLMs with external tools (like Aider for coding and Semantic Scholar for literature search) enables robust, iterative experimentation and knowledge synthesis.

Automated LLM-based peer reviewers can achieve near-human-level accuracy in evaluating scientific papers, offering a scalable solution for quality control and feedback in an autonomous research ecosystem.
originalProblem: Traditional human-driven scientific discovery is slow, labor-intensive, and limited by human ingenuity and time, hindering the pace of knowledge generation.
Existing AI applications in science primarily assist human researchers or automate constrained tasks, lacking an end-to-end framework for fully autonomous research.
There is a need to democratize scientific research by lowering financial and intellectual barriers, accelerating progress across various domains.
results: The AI Scientist generated hundreds of medium-quality papers at an approximate cost of $10-$15 per paper, leveraging LLM API costs for coding and writing.
A GPT-4o-based automated reviewer achieved near-human-level balanced accuracy (0.65) and a superhuman F1 Score (0.57) compared to human reviewers on ICLR 2022 papers, costing $0.25-$0.50 per review.

The system successfully implemented complex algorithmic changes and generated novel visualizations in case studies (e.g., 'Adaptive Dual-Scale Denoising'), though it exhibited issues like subtle code errors, hallucinations, and a positive interpretation bias.
solution: An end-to-end framework called "The AI Scientist" was developed, leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform iterative cycles of idea generation, experimental iteration, and scientific paper write-up.
The system integrates tools like Aider for autonomous code generation and execution, and connects to the Semantic Scholar API for novelty checks, related work, and citation management.
An LLM-based automated reviewer mimics conference guidelines to evaluate generated papers, providing numerical scores and textual feedback that can loop back into the discovery process.
paper_summary.summary: Sakana AI and collaborating institutions developed a comprehensive framework for fully automated scientific discovery, enabling AI agents to conduct an entire research endeavor from hypothesis to peer-reviewed paper. This system generated hundreds of medium-quality papers in machine learning at a cost of under $15 per paper, with its automated reviewer achieving near-human-level performance.

title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery

abstract: While AI systems demonstrate exponentially improving capabilities, the pace of AI research itself remains linearly bounded by human cognitive capacity, creating an increasingly severe development bottleneck. We present ASI-Arch, the first demonstration of Artificial Superintelligence for AI research (ASI4AI) in the critical domain of neural architecture discovery--a fully autonomous system that shatters this fundamental constraint by enabling AI to conduct its own architectural innovation. Moving beyond traditional Neural Architecture Search (NAS), which is fundamentally limited to exploring human-defined spaces, we introduce a paradigm shift from automated optimization to automated innovation. ASI-Arch can conduct end-to-end scientific research in the domain of architecture discovery, autonomously hypothesizing novel architectural concepts, implementing them as executable code, training and empirically validating their performance through rigorous experimentation and past experience. ASI-Arch conducted 1,773 autonomous experiments over 20,000 GPU hours, culminating in the discovery of 106 innovative, state-of-the-art (SOTA) linear attention architectures. Like AlphaGo's Move 37 that revealed unexpected strategic insights invisible to human players, our AI-discovered architectures demonstrate emergent design principles that systematically surpass human-designed baselines and illuminate previously unknown pathways for architectural innovation. Crucially, we establish the first empirical scaling law for scientific discovery itself--demonstrating that architectural breakthroughs can be scaled computationally, transforming research progress from a human-limited to a computation-scalable process. We provide comprehensive analysis of the emergent design patterns and autonomous research capabilities that enabled these breakthroughs, establishing a blueprint for self-accelerating AI systems.

keyInsights: Architectural breakthroughs can be scaled computationally, evidenced by a linear relationship between the cumulative count of discovered SOTA architectures and total computing hours consumed.

AI-discovered architectures exhibit 'emergent design principles' and 'Move 37' moments, revealing novel strategies that systematically surpass human intuition, such as breaking traditional trade-offs in gating mechanisms.

Top-performing AI-discovered architectures rely more on insights derived from the AI's own empirical 'analysis' (44.8% of design components) than on 'cognition' from human literature (48.6%), indicating a deeper level of understanding from self-experimentation.
originalProblem: The pace of AI research is linearly bounded by human cognitive capacity, creating an increasingly severe development bottleneck despite exponential improvements in AI capabilities.

Traditional Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods are confined to optimizing within human-defined search spaces, lacking the ability for true innovation or hypothesizing novel architectural concepts.
Designing efficient neural architectures, particularly for complex attention mechanisms, is a challenging and time-consuming task for human experts.
results: ASI-ARCH autonomously discovered 106 novel, state-of-the-art linear attention architectures, consuming approximately 20,000 GPU hours, which are publicly available.

Five top-performing AI-discovered architectures consistently outperformed human-designed baselines like DeltaNet, Gated DeltaNet, and Mamba2 on various language modeling and common-sense reasoning benchmarks.

The system established the 'first empirical scaling law for scientific discovery,' demonstrating a strong linear relationship between computational resources consumed and the rate of SOTA architectural discoveries.
solution: ASI-ARCH is a closed-loop evolutionary system comprising three interconnected LLM-driven modules: the Researcher (proposes novel architectures), the Engineer (implements and empirically evaluates), and the Analyst (synthesizes results and extracts insights).

It employs an 'exploration-then-verification' strategy to efficiently identify promising candidates with smaller models and then rigorously validate them at scale, using a robust self-revision mechanism for iterative debugging.
A comprehensive fitness function combines quantitative performance with qualitative architectural quality (scored by an LLM-as-judge) to drive the evolutionary improvement strategy.
paper_summary.summary: ASI-ARCH, developed by Shanghai Jiao Tong University and collaborators, is an autonomous system that discovers novel neural architectures by leveraging large language models for design, implementation, and analysis. It successfully identified 106 state-of-the-art linear attention architectures that outperform human-designed baselines and demonstrated the first empirical scaling law for scientific discovery, showing that architectural breakthroughs can be scaled computationally.

title: AlphaGo Moment for Model Architecture Discovery

abstract: Recent advances in deep-research systems have demonstrated the potential for AI agents to autonomously discover and synthesize knowledge from external sources. In this paper, we introduce WebResearcher, a novel framework for building such agents through two key components: (1) WebResearcher, an iterative deep-research paradigm that reformulates deep research as a Markov Decision Process, where agents periodically consolidate findings into evolving reports while maintaining focused workspaces, overcoming the context suffocation and noise contamination that plague existing mono-contextual approaches; and (2) WebFrontier, a scalable data synthesis engine that generates high-quality training data through tool-augmented complexity escalation, enabling systematic creation of research tasks that bridge the gap between passive knowledge recall and active knowledge construction. Notably, we find that the training data from our paradigm significantly enhances tool-use capabilities even for traditional mono-contextual methods. Furthermore, our paradigm naturally scales through parallel thinking, enabling concurrent multi-agent exploration for more comprehensive conclusions. Extensive experiments across 6 challenging benchmarks demonstrate that WebResearcher achieves state-of-the-art performance, even surpassing frontier proprietary systems.

keyInsights: Redefining deep research as a Markov Decision Process with iterative synthesis and workspace reconstruction effectively mitigates context degradation and error propagation.
High-quality training data, generated by systematically escalating cognitive complexity with tool use, is essential and can significantly enhance agent capabilities.
Leveraging a multi-agent Research-Synthesis Framework at inference time improves robustness and accuracy by integrating diverse reasoning paths.
originalProblem: Existing deep-research AI agents suffer from "cognitive workspace suffocation" due to continuously expanding context windows in their mono-contextual paradigm.
Irreversible noise contamination and accumulation of irrelevant information degrade reasoning quality over long research horizons.
There is a critical scarcity of high-quality, complex training data for developing robust tool-augmented deep-research agents.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance across six challenging benchmarks, outperforming larger general LLMs and existing proprietary/open-source deep-research systems.
Secured 36.7% accuracy on Humanity's Last Exam, representing a 6.9 percentage point lead over the next best system, and 75.7% accuracy on GAIA, a 9.7 percentage point improvement over DeepSeek-V3.1.
Ablation studies confirmed the Iterative Deep-Research Paradigm as the critical performance driver, and the Research-Synthesis Framework improved HLE pass@1 from 28.8% to 35.65% with parallel agents.
solution: Introduction of IterResearch, an iterative deep-research paradigm that reformulates research as a Markov Decision Process, enabling periodic synthesis of findings and reconstruction of focused workspaces.
Development of WebFrontier, a scalable data synthesis engine, which generates large volumes of high-quality, complex, and verifiable training data through a multi-agent iterative workflow.
Implementation of a Research-Synthesis Framework at test-time, utilizing parallel research agents and a synthesis agent to integrate diverse findings for more comprehensive and robust conclusions.
paper_summary.summary: WebResearcher, an AI agent framework from Alibaba Group's Tongyi Lab, introduces an iterative deep-research paradigm and a scalable data synthesis engine to overcome context limitations in long-horizon tasks. It demonstrated state-of-the-art performance across six challenging benchmarks, achieving a 6.9 percentage point improvement on Humanity's Last Exam and a 9.7 percentage point lead on GAIA over previous top systems.

title: WebResearcher: Unleashing unbounded reasoning capability in Long-Horizon Agents

abstract:
We present the AI Cosmologist, an agentic system designed to automate
cosmological/astronomical data analysis and machine learning research
workflows. This implements a complete pipeline from idea generation to
experimental evaluation and research dissemination, mimicking the scientific
process typically performed by human researchers. The system employs
specialized agents for planning, coding, execution, analysis, and synthesis
that work together to develop novel approaches. Unlike traditional auto
machine-learning systems, the AI Cosmologist generates diverse implementation
strategies, writes complete code, handles execution errors, analyzes results,
and synthesizes new approaches based on experimental outcomes. We demonstrate
the AI Cosmologist capabilities across several machine learning tasks, showing
how it can successfully explore solution spaces, iterate based on experimental
results, and combine successful elements from different approaches. Our results
indicate that agentic systems can automate portions of the research process,
potentially accelerating scientific discovery. The code and experimental data
used in this paper are available on GitHub at
this https URL Example papers included in the
appendix demonstrate the system's capability to autonomously produce complete
scientific publications, starting from only the dataset and task description

keyInsights: Breaking down complex research workflows into specialized agent roles enables effective automation while maintaining scientific rigor
Iterative refinement through agent collaboration and reflection mechanisms leads to improved solutions
Integration of physics domain knowledge with deep learning approaches yields superior results
originalProblem: Growing cosmological datasets require automated analysis approaches but current AutoML solutions lack creative problem-solving capabilities
Machine learning deployment demands significant domain expertise and iterative experimentation, creating research bottlenecks
results: Exceeded original competition winner's performance on Galaxy Zoo 2 morphology classification task
Achieved state-of-the-art parameter constraints on Quijote cosmological simulations, particularly for challenging Ωb and h parameters
Generated complete research manuscripts with visualizations and bibliography in LaTeX/BibTeX format
Completed research cycles in hours/days compared to typical weeks/months timeframe
solution: Modular agentic system with specialized components for different research stages, coordinated through conditional execution framework
Two-phase approach combining research (implementation/experimentation) and dissemination (manuscript generation) capabilities
paper_summary.summary: University of Nottingham researchers introduce an autonomous agentic system that automates end-to-end machine learning research in cosmology, incorporating specialized agents for planning, coding, execution, analysis and synthesis to achieve state-of-the-art performance on galaxy morphology classification and cosmological parameter inference tasks while generating publication-ready manuscripts.

title: The AI Cosmologist I: An Agentic System for Automated Data Analysis

abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is accelerating the transformation of scientific research paradigms, not only enhancing research efficiency but also driving innovation. We introduce InternAgent, a unified closed-loop multi-agent framework to conduct Autonomous Scientific Research (ASR) across various scientific research fields, enabling researchers to tackle complicated problems in these fields with unprecedented speed and precision. InternAgent highlights three key advantages: 1) Scalability: InternAgent has demonstrated its versatility across 12 scientific research tasks, capable of generating innovative ideas to enhance the performance of baseline code. 2) Interactivity: InternAgent provides an interface for human expert feedback and multi-agent interaction in automated end-to-end processes, allowing for the seamless integration of domain expert knowledge. 3) Efficiency: InternAgent has achieved promising performance gains in several scientific fields with significantly less time cost compared to human efforts. For instance, in reaction yield prediction, it increased from 27.6% to 35.4% in just 12 hours; in enhancer activity prediction, accuracy rose from 0.65 to 0.79 with only 4 hours of processing; and in 2D semantic segmentation, precision advanced from 78.8% to 81.0% in a mere 30 hours.

keyInsights: A multi-agent architecture with specialized, collaborating roles can effectively automate the complex, iterative nature of scientific discovery, from literature review to experimental refinement.
Integrating human-interactive feedback and self-evolving idea generation mechanisms is crucial for fostering novelty and scientific validity in AI-generated hypotheses.

The ability to support repository-level code and an adaptive experimental evolution process is vital for enabling AI to tackle complex, real-world scientific problems beyond simple, single-file tasks.
originalProblem: Autonomous systems struggle to generate effective and novel research proposals that balance creativity with scientific rigor.
A major hurdle in ASR is the seamless, closed-loop integration of experiment design, execution, result analysis, and iterative hypothesis refinement across diverse domains.
results: InternAgent consistently improved performance over baselines and existing auto-research systems across all 12 diverse scientific and AI tasks, achieving a maximum R² of 35.4% in Reaction Yield Prediction and 95.5% Overall Accuracy in 3D Point Cloud Classification.

It demonstrated cost-efficiency, with idea generation averaging around $0.6 per idea and complex repository-level debugging approximately $1.2 per run, making it significantly more economical than some prior systems (e.g., one-sixth the cost of AI-Researcher for AutoRYP).

Human evaluators (Ph.D.s or candidates) consistently rated InternAgent-generated ideas higher than those from AI-Scientist-V2 in terms of soundness, contribution, and overall quality, confirming its ability to produce effective and novel research concepts.
solution: A unified closed-loop multi-agent framework is employed, featuring specialized agents for self-evolving idea generation with human-interactive feedback (Survey, Code Review, Idea Innovation, Assessment, Orchestration Agents).
A comprehensive idea-to-methodology construction process systematically bridges abstract research ideas to concrete, implementable methodologies (Methodology Development Agent).

An evolutionary experimental planning and execution capability utilizes an Exception-Guided Debugging Framework and Adaptive Evolution for converting theoretical concepts into functional, iteratively refined code.
paper_summary.summary: The InternAgent framework establishes a unified closed-loop multi-agent system for autonomous scientific research, automating the entire cycle from hypothesis generation to experimental validation. It achieves substantial performance gains and cost-efficiency across 12 varied scientific and AI tasks, including handling complex repository-level codes.

title: InternAgent: When Agent Becomes the Scientist -- Building Closed-Loop System from Hypothesis to Verification

abstract: We introduce Paper2Agent, an automated framework that converts research papers into AI agents. Paper2Agent transforms research output from passive artifacts into active systems that can accelerate downstream use, adoption, and discovery. Conventional research papers require readers to invest substantial effort to understand and adapt a paper's code, data, and methods to their own work, creating barriers to dissemination and reuse. Paper2Agent addresses this challenge by automatically converting a paper into an AI agent that acts as a knowledgeable research assistant. It systematically analyzes the paper and the associated codebase using multiple agents to construct a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, then iteratively generates and runs tests to refine and robustify the resulting MCP. These paper MCPs can then be flexibly connected to a chat agent (e.g. Claude Code) to carry out complex scientific queries through natural language while invoking tools and workflows from the original paper. We demonstrate Paper2Agent's effectiveness in creating reliable and capable paper agents through in-depth case studies. Paper2Agent created an agent that leverages AlphaGenome to interpret genomic variants and agents based on ScanPy and TISSUE to carry out single-cell and spatial transcriptomics analyses. We validate that these paper agents can reproduce the original paper's results and can correctly carry out novel user queries. Paper2Agent automatically created AI co-scientist that identified new splicing variant associated with ADHD risk. By turning static papers into dynamic, interactive AI agents, Paper2Agent introduces a new paradigm for knowledge dissemination and a foundation for the collaborative ecosystem of AI co-scientists.

keyInsights: Transforming papers into interactive AI agents, capable of executing methods via natural language, significantly lowers technical barriers and democratizes access to advanced scientific tools.
An automated, multi-agent system leveraging the Model Context Protocol (MCP) can reliably synthesize executable tools from research codebases, validating them against original paper results.
AI co-scientists, formed by connecting multiple paper agents, can autonomously generate hypotheses, design and execute complex analyses, and contribute to novel scientific discoveries.
originalProblem: Traditional research papers and associated codebases present significant technical barriers (e.g., setup, dependencies) for readers, limiting reuse and adoption of computational methods.
Scientific reproducibility is hindered by the static nature of published research, making it difficult for non-expert scientists to understand, replicate, and apply methods.
The current paradigm of scientific communication is passive, lacking interactive tools that could accelerate downstream use and novel scientific discovery.
results: The AlphaGenome agent achieved 100.0% accuracy on genomic data interpretation queries (15/15 tutorial, 15/15 novel), outperforming other agentic systems (e.g., Claude + Repo, 60.0-80.0% accuracy).
Computational efficiency improved by median factors of 1.8x to 4.6x compared to alternative agentic systems for genomic analysis tasks.
An AI co-scientist analyzed 39 ADHD GWAS loci in two hours, prioritizing a novel causal variant (rs1626703) and elucidating its predicted mechanism, a process that typically takes weeks manually.
solution: Paper2Agent employs a multi-agent AI system, orchestrated by Claude Code, to automatically convert papers and code into Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers.
MCP servers expose structured APIs, tools, and resources (code, data, manuscript text) in a format accessible to Large Language Models (LLMs), along with guiding prompts for scientific workflows.
Specialized sub-agents handle environment configuration, tutorial discovery, tool synthesis from code, and iterative testing/refinement to ensure reliability and reproducibility.
paper_summary.summary: Paper2Agent is an automated framework that transforms scientific papers with code into interactive AI agents, making complex methods accessible via natural language. The system generated agents that achieved 100% accuracy on genomic analysis tasks, autonomously interpreted GWAS loci, and accelerated discovery in ADHD research by performing analyses in hours that typically take weeks.

title: Paper2Agent: Reimagining Research Papers As Interactive and Reliable AI Agents

abstract: While previous AI Scientist systems can generate novel findings, they often lack the focus to produce scientifically valuable contributions that address pressing human-defined challenges. We introduce DeepScientist, a system designed to overcome this by conducting goal-oriented, fully autonomous scientific discovery over month-long timelines. It formalizes discovery as a Bayesian Optimization problem, operationalized through a hierarchical evaluation process consisting of "hypothesize, verify, and analyze". Leveraging a cumulative Findings Memory, this loop intelligently balances the exploration of novel hypotheses with exploitation, selectively promoting the most promising findings to higher-fidelity levels of validation. Consuming over 20,000 GPU hours, the system generated about 5,000 unique scientific ideas and experimentally validated approximately 1100 of them, ultimately surpassing human-designed state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on three frontier AI tasks by 183.7\%, 1.9\%, and 7.9\%. This work provides the first large-scale evidence of an AI achieving discoveries that progressively surpass human SOTA on scientific tasks, producing valuable findings that genuinely push the frontier of scientific discovery. To facilitate further research into this process, we will open-source all experimental logs and system code at this https URL.

keyInsights: Despite a low success rate of 1-3% for generated ideas, DeepScientist's ability to conduct massive-scale trial-and-error at speed redefines the bottleneck in autonomous science from 'Can AI innovate?' to 'How can we efficiently guide its powerful, yet highly dissipative, exploratory process to maximize scientific return?'
DeepScientist exhibited a purposeful and progressive trajectory in its conceptual search, demonstrating an ability to deepen understanding and reorient its search after initial breakthroughs.

A near-linear relationship exists between parallel computational resources and the output of valuable scientific discoveries, driven by the synergistic effect of a shared 'Findings Memory' across parallel exploration paths.
originalProblem: Prior AI Scientist systems often produced 'undirected discoveries' lacking genuine scientific value or focus on human-defined challenges, primarily recombining existing knowledge rather than pushing frontiers.
Scientific discovery is a slow, iterative process, requiring extensive trial-and-error and continuous refinement over long periods by human researchers.
The full cycle of evaluating a scientific method is prohibitively expensive, making brute-force exploration intractable for complex problems.
results: DeepScientist developed the A2P method, improving agent failure attribution accuracy by 142.8% (+17.24) in handcraft settings and 183.7% (+30.79) in algorithm-generated settings.

The system achieved a 1.9% throughput improvement (+3.65 tokens/second) for LLM inference acceleration with its ACRA method and established a new SOTA for AI text detection with a 7.9% higher AUROC (+0.063) while doubling inference speed.

DeepScientist generated over 5,000 unique scientific ideas, leading to 21 'Progress Findings' over a month-long cycle, demonstrating its ability to compress years of human exploration into weeks using approximately 20,000 GPU hours.
solution: DeepScientist models scientific discovery as a goal-driven Bayesian Optimization problem, efficiently navigating the search space by constructing a surrogate model and balancing exploration with exploitation.
A multi-agent system, integrated with an open-knowledge base and a continuously accumulating 'Findings Memory' of both human and system-generated knowledge, guides subsequent explorations.

A hierarchical and iterative three-stage exploration cycle (Strategize & Hypothesize, Implement & Verify, Analyze & Report) filters ideas, ensuring only promising research is advanced to more expensive evaluations.
paper_summary.summary: DeepScientist, developed by WestlakeNLP, is an autonomous AI system that successfully surpassed human state-of-the-art on three frontier AI tasks within a month-long cycle, including a 142.8% improvement in agent failure attribution and a 7.9% higher AUROC for AI text detection. The system employs a goal-driven Bayesian optimization approach and a multi-agent architecture to conduct large-scale, iterative scientific exploration and validate findings.

title: DeepScientist: Advancing Frontier-Pushing Scientific Findings Progressively

abstract: Automated AI research holds great potential to accelerate scientific discovery. However, current LLMs often generate plausible-looking but ineffective ideas. Execution grounding may help, but it is unclear whether automated execution is feasible and whether LLMs can learn from the execution feedback. To investigate these, we first build an automated executor to implement ideas and launch large-scale parallel GPU experiments to verify their effectiveness. We then convert two realistic research problems - LLM pre-training and post-training - into execution environments and demonstrate that our automated executor can implement a large fraction of the ideas sampled from frontier LLMs. We analyze two methods to learn from the execution feedback: evolutionary search and reinforcement learning. Execution-guided evolutionary search is sample-efficient: it finds a method that significantly outperforms the GRPO baseline (69.4% vs 48.0%) on post-training, and finds a pre-training recipe that outperforms the nanoGPT baseline (19.7 minutes vs 35.9 minutes) on pre-training, all within just ten search epochs. Frontier LLMs often generate meaningful algorithmic ideas during search, but they tend to saturate early and only occasionally exhibit scaling trends. Reinforcement learning from execution reward, on the other hand, suffers from mode collapse. It successfully improves the average reward of the ideator model but not the upper-bound, due to models converging on simple ideas. We thoroughly analyze the executed ideas and training dynamics to facilitate future efforts towards execution-grounded automated AI research.

keyInsights: A fully automated execution loop for open-ended LLM-generated research ideas is feasible, with frontier LLMs achieving high execution rates (e.g., >90% for Claude-4.5-Opus).
Execution-guided evolutionary search effectively leverages feedback, allowing LLMs to generate genuinely effective algorithmic ideas that sometimes rediscover recent research concepts.

Standard reinforcement learning (GRPO) from execution reward leads to diversity collapse and stagnation in maximum reward, indicating its limitations for open-ended scientific discovery where novelty is key.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at generating plausible research ideas but often lack empirical validation, leading to an 'ideation-execution gap'.
Automated AI research lacks scalable, high-throughput systems to implement and test open-ended, LLM-generated algorithmic ideas.
Uncertainty about whether LLMs can effectively learn from execution feedback to generate more potent and effective research contributions.
results: Execution-Guided Evolutionary Search (ES) achieved 69.4% validation accuracy on the post-training task, surpassing the best human expert solution of 68.8%.
ES reduced the wall-clock time required to pre-train nanoGPT to a target validation loss from a baseline of 35.9 minutes to 19.7 minutes.
Reinforcement Learning increased the average reward of generated ideas but caused a severe diversity collapse and no sustained improvement in the maximum reward.
solution: Built a high-throughput, automated idea executor comprising an LLM-based implementer (for code generation), a scheduler, and a GPU cluster worker for parallel experiment execution.
Established two challenging, open-ended research environments for LLM ideation: improving nanoGPT pre-training time and optimizing GRPO post-training accuracy.
Implemented and evaluated two learning algorithms from execution feedback: Execution-Guided Evolutionary Search (ES) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) with GRPO, using frontier LLMs as ideators.
paper_summary.summary: Stanford University researchers developed an automated system for AI research, enabling Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate, implement, and empirically validate research ideas. Their execution-guided evolutionary search method achieved 69.4% validation accuracy on a post-training task, surpassing human expert performance, and reduced LLM pre-training time by over 45%.

title: Towards Execution-Grounded Automated AI Research

abstract: The increasing adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) has enabled AI scientists to perform complex end-to-end scientific discovery tasks requiring coordination of specialized roles, including idea generation and experimental execution. However, most state-of-the-art AI scientist systems rely on static, hand-designed pipelines and fail to adapt based on accumulated interaction histories. As a result, these systems overlook promising research directions, repeat failed experiments, and pursue infeasible ideas. To address this, we introduce EvoScientist, an evolving multi-agent AI scientist framework that continuously improves research strategies through persistent memory and self-evolution. EvoScientist comprises three specialized agents: a Researcher Agent (RA) for scientific idea generation, an Engineer Agent (EA) for experiment implementation and execution, and an Evolution Manager Agent (EMA) that distills insights from prior interactions into reusable knowledge. EvoScientist contains two persistent memory modules: (i) an ideation memory, which summarizes feasible research directions from top-ranked ideas while recording previously unsuccessful directions; and (ii) an experimentation memory, which captures effective data processing and model training strategies derived from code search trajectories and best-performing implementations. These modules enable the RA and EA to retrieve relevant prior strategies, improving idea quality and code execution success rates over time. Experiments show that EvoScientist outperforms 7 open-source and commercial state-of-the-art systems in scientific idea generation, achieving higher novelty, feasibility, relevance, and clarity via automatic and human evaluation. EvoScientist also substantially improves code execution success rates through multi-agent evolution, demonstrating persistent memory's effectiveness for end-to-end scientific discovery.

keyInsights: Scientific discovery can be framed as a continuous learning problem, where multi-agent systems adapt and improve strategies based on accumulated outcomes and failures.
The integration of propose-review-refine idea tree search and Elo-based tournament selection significantly enhances the novelty, feasibility, relevance, and clarity of generated scientific ideas.
Distilling insights from code generation trajectories and experimental results into experimentation memory measurably increases the mean execution success rate of experimental code.
originalProblem: Existing AI scientist systems operate with static execution pipelines, preventing continuous adaptation and improvement from accumulated experience.
Current AI agents for scientific discovery often pursue known failure patterns or overlook promising research directions due to fixed decision strategies and interaction patterns.
The absence of a mechanism for AI scientists to evolve their idea generation and experimental execution strategies over time by learning from prior successes and failures.
results: EvoScientist consistently outperformed 7 open-source and commercial baselines in idea generation, with average win rates ranging from +29.17% to +93.34% against open-sourced systems in novelty, feasibility, relevance, and clarity.
The system achieved a measurable improvement in code execution success rate, increasing the average from 34.39% to 44.56% across four experiment stages after Experiment Strategy Evolution.

Six complete research papers autonomously generated by EvoScientist were accepted to the International Conference on AI Scientists (ICAIS 2025), which had an overall acceptance rate of 31.71%, with one paper receiving a Best Paper Award.
solution: A self-evolving multi-agent system framework, EvoScientist, was designed with specialized Researcher, Engineer, and Evolution Manager Agents.
Two persistent memory modules, Ideation Memory and Experimentation Memory, store distilled insights from feasible research directions and effective execution strategies.

Multi-agent self-evolution mechanisms (Idea Direction Evolution, Idea Validation Evolution, Experiment Strategy Evolution) enable the system to learn from interaction histories and update memory modules for continuous improvement.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. developed EvoScientist, a multi-agent framework that enables AI scientists to continuously improve their idea generation and experiment execution strategies by learning from past outcomes. The system produced higher-quality scientific ideas and demonstrated increased code execution success rates, leading to accepted research papers at an AI conference, including a Best Paper Award.

title: EvoScientist: Towards Multi-Agent Evolving AI Scientists for End-to-End Scientific Discovery

abstract:
The powerful reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in
mathematics and coding, combined with their ability to automate complex tasks
through agentic frameworks, present unprecedented opportunities for
accelerating scientific innovation. In this paper, we introduce AI-Researcher,
a fully autonomous research system that transforms how AI-driven scientific
discovery is conducted and evaluated. Our framework seamlessly orchestrates the
complete research pipeline--from literature review and hypothesis generation to
algorithm implementation and publication-ready manuscript preparation--with
minimal human intervention. To rigorously assess autonomous research
capabilities, we develop Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising
state-of-the-art papers across diverse AI research domains, featuring both
guided innovation and open-ended exploration tasks. Through extensive
experiments, we demonstrate that AI-Researcher achieves remarkable
implementation success rates and produces research papers that approach
human-level quality. This work establishes new foundations for autonomous
scientific innovation that can complement human researchers by systematically
exploring solution spaces beyond cognitive limitations.

keyInsights: Fully autonomous AI systems can orchestrate the entire scientific discovery pipeline, from conceptualization to publication-ready manuscript generation, with high fidelity.

Open-ended autonomous exploration tasks (Level-2) can yield superior scientific quality compared to instruction-guided tasks (Level-1), suggesting that prescriptive instructions might constrain AI creativity.

The choice of the foundational LLM backbone significantly impacts both the implementation success rates and the scientific quality of the generated research, with Claude-series models often outperforming GPT-4o for this task.
originalProblem: Human researchers face limitations (cognitive biases, processing capacity) in navigating the vast and complex landscape of scientific discovery.
Existing AI agent systems, while automating routine tasks, lack the deep intellectual capacity and end-to-end orchestration needed for genuine scientific innovation.
A critical absence of standardized benchmarks hinders systematic evaluation and progress in autonomous scientific research systems.
results: AI-Researcher achieved a 93.8% code implementation completeness rate with Claude-series models on Scientist-Bench, successfully producing executable code for diverse tasks.
AI-generated papers approached human-level quality, with 15.79% to 78.95% (depending on the LLM evaluator) deemed comparable to human-authored top-tier research.

The system demonstrated superior scientific quality in open-ended (Level-2) innovation tasks, achieving higher average ratings and comparable rates (e.g., 40-100% comparable) than instruction-guided (Level-1) tasks.
solution: Introduces AI-Researcher, a multi-agent framework that autonomously orchestrates the complete scientific discovery lifecycle, from literature review and hypothesis generation to algorithm implementation, experimental validation, and manuscript preparation.
Develops a Multi-Stage Refinement Architecture and Expert Validation Framework that mimics human iterative research, enhancing implementation success rates and allowing for test-time scaling.

Presents Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring Level-1 (guided innovation) and Level-2 (autonomous exploration) tasks, along with a two-stage evaluation protocol for technical execution and scientific contribution.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The University of Hong Kong developed AI-Researcher, a fully autonomous AI system designed to conduct end-to-end scientific discovery, and introduced Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating autonomous research capabilities. The system achieved high code implementation rates and generated research papers that approached human-level quality, particularly excelling in open-ended scientific innovation tasks.

title: AI-Researcher: Autonomous Scientific Innovation

abstract: This paper introduces Agent-Based Auto Research, a structured multi-agent framework designed to automate, coordinate, and optimize the full lifecycle of scientific research. Leveraging the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and modular agent collaboration, the system spans all major research phases, including literature review, ideation, methodology planning, experimentation, paper writing, peer review response, and dissemination. By addressing issues such as fragmented workflows, uneven methodological expertise, and cognitive overload, the framework offers a systematic and scalable approach to scientific inquiry. Preliminary explorations demonstrate the feasibility and potential of Auto Research as a promising paradigm for self-improving, AI-driven research processes.

keyInsights: The work redefines scientific inquiry as a modular, interpretable, and optimizable process, enabling a systemic integration of human and machine intelligence.
LLM agents can effectively coordinate and automate complex research tasks across the full lifecycle, from generating novel ideas to strategizing research dissemination.
This framework promises to democratize research access, accelerate scientific progress, and enhance reproducibility by mitigating human limitations and cognitive overload.
originalProblem: Traditional scientific research suffers from fragmented workflows, leading to inefficiencies and increased cognitive burden for researchers.
There is an uneven distribution of methodological expertise and limited systematization in problem-solving, impacting research quality and reproducibility.
Researchers experience cognitive overload due to the vast amount of information and diverse skill sets required, hindering deep engagement with core problems.
results: LLM agents successfully clarified research topics, identified gaps in literature, and generated initial LaTeX drafts for literature reviews.
A prototype method planner generated logical research plans, and a heuristic solution designer selected practical techniques for sub-tasks, such as using LSTM for cryptomining detection.

Experimental agents correctly identified appropriate benchmarks for 90% of CV/NLP tasks, aligned baselines with human selections in 78% of cases, and produced executable Python code (72-78% unmodified).
solution: A multi-agent framework is introduced, employing specialized LLM agents for each of the eight phases of the research lifecycle (Literature, Idea, Method, Experiment, Paper, Evaluation, Rebuttal, Promotion).
Agents leverage LLMs for natural language understanding, content generation, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning to decompose problems and make heuristic-driven decisions.
The system dynamically retrieves information from real-time search engines and scholarly databases to ensure up-to-date knowledge and supports iterative refinement and self-optimization.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nanyang Technological University and Nankai University propose an "Agent-Based Auto Research" framework, which utilizes a multi-agent system powered by Large Language Models to automate the entire scientific inquiry lifecycle. Preliminary explorations across eight research phases demonstrate the feasibility of agents managing tasks from literature review and ideation to experimental setup, paper drafting, peer review simulation, and research dissemination.

title: A Vision for Auto Research with LLM Agents

abstract: Can AI accelerate the development of AI itself? While recent agentic systems have shown strong performance on well-scoped tasks with rapid feedback, it remains unclear whether they can tackle the costly, long-horizon, and weakly supervised research loops that drive real AI progress. We present ASI-Evolve, an agentic framework for AI-for-AI research that closes this loop through a learn-design-experiment-analyze cycle. ASI-Evolve augments standard evolutionary agents with two key components: a cognition base that injects accumulated human priors into each round of exploration, and a dedicated analyzer that distills complex experimental outcomes into reusable insights for future iterations. To our knowledge, ASI-Evolve is the first unified framework to demonstrate AI-driven discovery across three central components of AI development: data, architectures, and learning algorithms. In neural architecture design, it discovered 105 SOTA linear attention architectures, with the best discovered model surpassing DeltaNet by +0.97 points, nearly 3x the gain of recent human-designed improvements. In pretraining data curation, the evolved pipeline improves average benchmark performance by +3.96 points, with gains exceeding 18 points on MMLU. In reinforcement learning algorithm design, discovered algorithms outperform GRPO by up to +12.5 points on AMC32, +11.67 points on AIME24, and +5.04 points on OlympiadBench. We further provide initial evidence that this AI-for-AI paradigm can transfer beyond the AI stack through experiments in mathematics and biomedicine. Together, these results suggest that ASI-Evolve represents a promising step toward enabling AI to accelerate AI across the foundational stages of development, offering early evidence for the feasibility of closed-loop AI research.

keyInsights: AI can effectively perform complex, long-horizon scientific research tasks characterized by high execution cost, vast search spaces, and multi-dimensional feedback.
Integrating human prior knowledge and systematically accumulating internal experience significantly accelerates cold-start exploration and sustained improvements in AI-driven discovery.
A dedicated analysis component is critical for translating complex experimental outputs into structured, actionable insights, enabling continuous and reliable evolution over many iterations.
originalProblem: Traditional AI development is constrained by human limitations in exploring vast hypothesis spaces for new solutions.
Experimental workflows are demanding and require substantial manual effort, leading to high operational costs and slow iteration cycles.
Systematic accumulation and transfer of knowledge across research iterations are challenging, relying heavily on individual experience rather than automated processes.
results: In model architecture design, ASI-EVOLVE discovered architectures that achieved up to a +0.97 point gain over the DeltaNet baseline, surpassing recent human-designed state-of-the-art improvements by three times.
For pretraining data curation, AI-designed strategies improved benchmark scores by +3.96 points on average over raw data, outperforming established corpora like DCLM and FineWeb-Edu.

In reinforcement learning algorithm design, the system yielded algorithms that achieved up to +12.5 points improvement on mathematical reasoning benchmarks over the GRPO baseline and demonstrated theoretical innovations for stable training.
solution: The ASI-EVOLVE framework implements a closed learn–design–experiment–analyze cycle, enabling autonomous AI research across diverse and complex tasks.
It incorporates a 'cognition store' to inject human prior knowledge, heuristics, and domain literature, guiding the AI's hypothesis generation from the outset.
A dedicated 'analyzer' module distills complex, multi-dimensional experimental feedback into structured, actionable insights that inform future design iterations without overwhelming context.
paper_summary.summary: ASI-EVOLVE, an agentic framework developed by researchers from SJTU, SII, and GAIR, enables AI to autonomously accelerate its own development by iteratively generating, experimenting with, and analyzing new ideas across model architectures, pretraining data, and reinforcement learning algorithms. The system achieved a +0.97 point gain over human-designed baselines in architecture design, a +3.96 point improvement on AI-curated pretraining data, and up to +12.5 points on mathematical reasoning benchmarks for evolved RL algorithms.

title: ASI-Evolve: AI Accelerates AI

abstract:
The automation of scientific discovery has been a long-standing goal within
the research community, driven by the potential to accelerate knowledge
creation. While significant progress has been made using commercial large
language models (LLMs) as research assistants or idea generators, the
possibility of automating the entire research process with open-source LLMs
remains largely unexplored. This paper explores the feasibility of using
open-source post-trained LLMs as autonomous agents capable of performing the
full cycle of automated research and review, from literature review and
manuscript preparation to peer review and paper refinement. Our iterative
preference training framework consists of CycleResearcher, which conducts
research tasks, and CycleReviewer, which simulates the peer review process,
providing iterative feedback via reinforcement learning. To train these models,
we develop two new datasets, Review-5k and Research-14k, reflecting real-world
machine learning research and peer review dynamics. Our results demonstrate
that CycleReviewer achieves promising performance with a 26.89\% reduction in
mean absolute error (MAE) compared to individual human reviewers in predicting
paper scores, indicating the potential of LLMs to effectively assist
expert-level research evaluation. In research, the papers generated by the
CycleResearcher model achieved a score of 5.36 in simulated peer reviews,
showing some competitiveness in terms of simulated review scores compared to
the preprint level of 5.24 from human experts, while still having room for
improvement compared to the accepted paper level of 5.69. This work represents
a significant step toward fully automated scientific inquiry, providing ethical
safeguards and exploring AI-driven research capabilities. The code, dataset and
model weight are released at this https URL

keyInsights: An iterative research-review-refinement loop, simulating human academic processes, effectively trains large language models for automated scientific inquiry.
Open-source large language models can be post-trained to achieve automated paper generation quality competitive with human preprints and provide consistent automated peer review.
Integrating ethical safeguards like AI content detection and disclosure is crucial for responsible deployment of AI in academic research workflows.
originalProblem: Full automation of the scientific research process, particularly with open-source Large Language Models (LLMs), remains largely unexplored.
Existing automated research pipelines lack an iterative feedback mechanism, such as simulated peer review, to refine and improve generated research outputs.
Concerns about academic integrity and potential misuse of LLMs for generating low-quality or undetectable AI content in scientific publications are increasing.
results: CycleReviewer achieved a 26.89% reduction in Proxy Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for review score consistency compared to individual human reviewers and exhibited competitive decision accuracy (74.24%).

Papers generated by CycleResearcher (12B variant) attained an average simulated peer review score of 5.36, competitive with human-written preprint papers (5.24 average score from human experts), and achieved a simulated acceptance rate of 35.13%.
The integrated Fast-DetectGPT tool demonstrated high accuracy in identifying AI-generated content: 95.14% for review content and nearly 99% for paper text.
solution: Developed CYCLERESEARCHER, an iterative preference training framework that mimics the Research-Review-Refinement cycle, built upon the Simple Preference Optimization (SimPO) method.
Introduced two specialized components: CycleResearcher, a policy model for scientific paper generation, and CycleReviewer, a reward model for simulating the peer review process.
Constructed two large-scale datasets, Review-5k and Research-14k, specifically designed for training the automated reviewer and research generator, respectively.
Integrated ethical safeguards, including a highly accurate AI-generated content detection tool and a framework for mandatory disclosure and licensing of AI-assisted publications.
paper_summary.summary: The CYCLERESEARCHER framework automates the full research-review-refinement cycle using open-source large language models, generating papers competitive with human preprints and providing automated review with high consistency. CycleReviewer demonstrated a 26.89% reduction in Proxy MAE for review score consistency, while CycleResearcher produced papers achieving an average simulated peer review score of 5.36, comparable to human-written preprints.

title: CycleResearcher: Improving Automated Research via Automated Review

abstract: The automation of scientific discovery represents a critical milestone in Artificial Intelligence (AI) research. However, existing agentic systems for science suffer from two fundamental limitations: rigid, pre-programmed workflows that cannot adapt to intermediate findings, and inadequate context management that hinders long-horizon research. We present \texttt{freephdlabor}, an open-source multiagent framework featuring \textit{fully dynamic workflows} determined by real-time agent reasoning and a \coloremph{\textit{modular architecture}} enabling seamless customization -- users can modify, add, or remove agents to address domain-specific requirements. The framework provides comprehensive infrastructure including \textit{automatic context compaction}, \textit{workspace-based communication} to prevent information degradation, \textit{memory persistence} across sessions, and \textit{non-blocking human intervention} mechanisms. These features collectively transform automated research from isolated, single-run attempts into \textit{continual research programs} that build systematically on prior explorations and incorporate human feedback. By providing both the architectural principles and practical implementation for building customizable co-scientist systems, this work aims to facilitate broader adoption of automated research across scientific domains, enabling practitioners to deploy interactive multiagent systems that autonomously conduct end-to-end research -- from ideation through experimentation to publication-ready manuscripts.

keyInsights: Dynamic workflows emerging from real-time agent reasoning enable true adaptability and responsiveness in scientific discovery, unlike rigid pre-programmed pipelines.
Robust, lossless communication via a shared file-based workspace is essential to prevent information degradation in complex, long-horizon multi-agent interactions.
A modular, open-source design significantly lowers barriers to entry, allowing scientists to customize AI agents for specific domain needs and fostering wider adoption.
originalProblem: Existing AI science automation systems rely on rigid, pre-programmed workflows, hindering adaptability to evolving research contexts or unexpected findings.
Large Language Models suffer from limited context windows, leading to information loss and distortion over iterative interactions in long-horizon scientific tasks.
Inter-agent communication in multi-agent systems often results in 'information degradation' or the 'game of telephone effect,' impeding reliable coordination.
results: Successfully completed an end-to-end research project on 'HMM-based Training Phase Detection,' from ideation to a publication-ready manuscript.
Demonstrated dynamic error recovery, where the ManagerAgent autonomously identified and resolved a workspace configuration issue without human intervention.

Achieved quality-driven iteration, utilizing a ReviewerAgent to assess paper drafts and orchestrating comprehensive revisions, ultimately improving the paper's quality to an 'Accept with Minor Revisions' equivalent.
solution: Employs a star-shaped multi-agent architecture with a central ManagerAgent that dynamically orchestrates tasks using the ReAct framework, adapting workflows in real-time.
Utilizes a shared, file-based workspace for inter-agent communication, ensuring lossless data exchange and acting as persistent external memory.
Incorporates features for continual research, including automatic context compaction, memory persistence, and non-blocking human intervention for long-term collaboration.
paper_summary.summary: freephdlabor introduces an open-source, multi-agent framework that orchestrates dynamic scientific research workflows, enabling AI agents to conduct continuous, interactive, and customizable end-to-end discovery. This system demonstrates its capability by autonomously completing a research project, including error recovery and quality-driven revisions, from ideation to a publication-ready manuscript.

title: Build Your Personalized Research Group: A Multiagent Framework for Continual and Interactive Science Automation

abstract: AI agents increasingly operate over extended time horizons, yet their ability to retain, organize, and recall multimodal experiences remains a critical bottleneck. Building effective lifelong memory requires navigating a vast design space spanning architecture, retrieval strategies, prompt engineering, and data pipelines; this space is too large and interconnected for manual exploration or traditional AutoML to explore effectively. We deploy an autonomous research pipeline to discover Omni-SimpleMem, a unified multimodal memory framework for lifelong AI agents. Starting from a naïve baseline (F1=0.117 on LoCoMo), the pipeline autonomously executes ${\sim}50$ experiments across two benchmarks, diagnosing failure modes, proposing architectural modifications, and repairing data pipeline bugs, all without human intervention in the inner loop. The resulting system achieves state-of-the-art on both benchmarks, improving F1 by +411% on LoCoMo (0.117$\to$0.598) and +214% on Mem-Gallery (0.254$\to$0.797) relative to the initial configurations. Critically, the most impactful discoveries are not hyperparameter adjustments: bug fixes (+175%), architectural changes (+44%), and prompt engineering (+188% on specific categories) each individually exceed the cumulative contribution of all hyperparameter tuning, demonstrating capabilities fundamentally beyond the reach of traditional AutoML. We provide a taxonomy of six discovery types and identify four properties that make multimodal memory particularly suited for autoresearch, offering guidance for applying autonomous research pipelines to other AI system domains. Code is available at this this https URL.

keyInsights: Autonomous research pipelines can effectively discover and optimize complex AI systems by performing architectural redesigns, diagnosing/fixing bugs (e.g., +175% F1 from one-line fix), and refining prompt engineering, extending beyond traditional hyperparameter tuning.
The most impactful performance gains in complex systems often stem from structural changes, bug fixes, and prompt refinements rather than cumulative hyperparameter adjustments.

Multimodal memory is particularly suitable for autonomous research due to immediate scalar evaluation metrics, a modular architecture, fast iteration cycles, and version-controlled code modifications.
originalProblem: AI agents struggle to effectively retain, organize, and recall the diverse, multimodal experiences accumulated during lifelong interactions.
Existing memory systems are often unimodal or fragmented, neglect rich visual and auditory signals, and face scalability or retrieval quality degradation.
Manual research for complex AI systems is slow and limited, while traditional AutoML cannot perform architectural redesigns or complex bug fixes crucial for optimization.
results: The autonomous pipeline successfully improved LoCoMo F1 from 0.117 to 0.598 (+411%) and Mem-Gallery F1 from 0.254 to 0.797 (+214%) over approximately 72 hours, demonstrating high-impact bug fixes and architectural discoveries.

OMNI-SIMPLEMEM achieved state-of-the-art F1 scores of up to 0.613 on LoCoMo and 0.810 on Mem-Gallery, consistently outperforming leading baselines by substantial margins (e.g., >25 percentage points on Mem-Gallery).
The system demonstrated 3.5 times faster throughput than the fastest baseline (5.81 queries/second with 8 workers) due to its architecture supporting concurrent lookups using read-only indices.
solution: An autonomous research pipeline, AUTORESEARCHCLAW, was deployed to iteratively discover and optimize OMNI-SIMPLEMEM, starting from a text-only memory framework.

The pipeline analyzes performance deficiencies, generates hypotheses, implements code changes (architectural, retrieval, prompt, data pipeline), executes experiments, and evaluates using quantitative metrics.

OMNI-SIMPLEMEM features selective novelty-based multimodal ingestion into Multimodal Atomic Units (MAUs), two-tier hot/cold storage, progressive pyramid retrieval with hybrid dense-sparse search, and knowledge graph-augmented retrieval.
paper_summary.summary: An autonomous research pipeline generated OMNI-SIMPLEMEM, a unified multimodal memory framework that enables lifelong AI agents to effectively manage diverse experiences across text, images, audio, and video. This system achieved state-of-the-art F1 scores up to 0.613 on LoCoMo and 0.810 on Mem-Gallery benchmarks, significantly outperforming previous baselines, while also demonstrating 3.5x faster throughput.

title: Omni-SimpleMem: Autoresearch-Guided Discovery of Lifelong Multimodal Agent Memory

abstract:
The scientific research paradigm is undergoing a profound transformation
owing to the development of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Recent works
demonstrate that various AI-assisted research methods can largely improve
research efficiency by improving data analysis, accelerating computation, and
fostering novel idea generation. To further move towards the ultimate goal
(i.e., automatic scientific research), in this paper, we introduce Dolphin, a
closed-loop LLM-driven framework to enhance the automation level of scientific
research. Dolphin first generates novel ideas based on feedback from previous
experiments and relevant papers ranked by the topic and task attributes. Then,
the generated ideas can be implemented using a code template refined and
debugged with the designed exception-traceback-guided local code structure.
Finally, Dolphin automatically analyzes the results of each idea and feeds the
results back to the next round of idea generation. Experiments are conducted on
the benchmark datasets of different topics and a subset of MLE-bench. Results
show that Dolphin can continuously improve the performance of the input topic
in a loop. We highlight that Dolphin can automatically propose methods that are
comparable to the state-of-the-art in some tasks such as 3D point
classification.

keyInsights: Integrating empirical practice and iterative feedback is essential for truly autonomous scientific discovery, moving beyond fragmented AI-assisted tools focused solely on thinking.
Careful filtering of reference papers based on task attributes and generated ideas for novelty and independence significantly improves the quality and efficiency of AI-proposed solutions.
Targeted debugging with extracted exception tracebacks and guided local code structure is crucial for substantially increasing the execution success rate of LLM-generated experimental code.
originalProblem: Existing auto-research efforts lack rigorous experimental validation on real-world, comparative datasets for AI-generated ideas, often relying on human judgment or simple, self-constructed data.
Prior AI-driven research frameworks frequently omit a crucial iterative feedback mechanism where experimental outcomes inform and refine subsequent idea generation.
LLM-generated code often contains errors, and the retrieval of irrelevant papers can misguide idea generation, reducing the overall efficiency of AI-driven research.
results: AI-generated ideas achieved an average improvement of +1.0% OA and a maximum of +2.9% OA on 3D Point Classification (ModelNet40), reaching 93.9% OA, comparable to human-designed state-of-the-art methods.

The framework demonstrated performance improvements across 2D image classification (CIFAR-100) and sentiment classification (SST-2), and successfully integrated with ML engineering tasks on MLE-bench.

Ablation studies confirmed that task-attribute-guided paper ranking boosted novel idea generation (19/20 novel ideas), while exception-traceback-guided debugging significantly increased code execution success rates. The closed-loop feedback mechanism progressively improved the idea improvement rate from 2/7 to 4/8 over iterations.
solution: A closed-loop LLM-driven framework, DOLPHIN, is introduced, comprising three interconnected stages: idea generation, experimental verification, and results feedback.
Idea generation incorporates a task-attribute-guided paper ranking system and a two-stage idea filtering procedure to ensure novelty and non-redundancy, drawing from high-quality literature.
