Experimental verification includes an exception-traceback-guided debugging mechanism that uses extracted traceback information and locally generated code structures to guide LLMs in error correction, while results feedback integrates outcomes to refine future idea generation prompts.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University and Shanghai AI Laboratory developed DOLPHIN, a closed-loop framework that automates scientific research by integrating iterative idea generation, empirical experimental verification, and results-driven feedback. This system generates AI-proposed methods that achieve performance comparable to or exceeding human-designed solutions on various established benchmarks, such as improving 3D point classification accuracy to 93.9% OA.

title: Dolphin: Moving Towards Closed-loop Auto-research through Thinking,  Practice, and Feedback

abstract:
Efficient experiment reproduction is critical to accelerating progress in
artificial intelligence. However, the inherent complexity of method design and
training procedures presents substantial challenges for automation. Notably,
reproducing experiments often requires implicit domain-specific knowledge not
explicitly documented in the original papers. To address this, we introduce the
paper lineage algorithm, which identifies and extracts implicit knowledge from
the relevant references cited by the target paper. Building on this idea, we
propose AutoReproduce, a multi-agent framework capable of automatically
reproducing experiments described in research papers in an end-to-end manner.
AutoReproduce enhances code executability by generating unit tests alongside
the reproduction process. To evaluate the reproduction capability, we construct
ReproduceBench, a benchmark annotated with verified implementations, and
introduce novel evaluation metrics to assess both the reproduction and
execution fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that AutoReproduce
outperforms the existing strong agent baselines on all five evaluation metrics
by a peak margin of over $70\%$. In particular, compared to the official
implementations, AutoReproduce achieves an average performance gap of $22.1\%$
on $89.74\%$ of the executable experiment runs. The code will be available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Incorporating knowledge from cited papers and their codebases through the 'paper lineage' algorithm is essential for inferring implicit implementation details and improving reproduction accuracy.

Iterative debugging and refinement processes, including unit testing with batch sampling and detailed data attribute understanding, are crucial for achieving high code executability and minimizing performance discrepancies.

A collaborative multi-agent LLM framework can successfully manage complex, end-to-end scientific tasks, moving beyond simple code generation to encompass comprehension, reasoning, and verification for scientific automation.
originalProblem: Efficient AI experiment reproduction is hampered by the inherent complexity of method design and training procedures, consuming significant time and specialized expertise.
Research papers often rely on 'implicit domain-specific knowledge' not explicitly documented, making accurate manual reproduction exceptionally difficult.

Existing LLM-driven automation tools primarily address discrete experimental stages or offer partial assistance, lacking comprehensive end-to-end frameworks for automatic reproduction with guaranteed executability and performance fidelity.
results: AUTOREPRODUCE achieved an 89.74% execution rate and a 30.13% performance gap on the REPRODUCEBENCH benchmark, significantly outperforming baselines like ChatDev (2.56% Exec Rate) and PaperCoder (34.46% Performance Gap).
Ablation studies confirm the paper lineage algorithm and the debugging and refinement processes are key contributors to improvements in both code alignment and performance gap metrics.

Human evaluations consistently rated AUTOREPRODUCE's generated code as higher quality, scoring substantially better across method (7.23 vs. 4.08), parameter (3.69 vs. 2.85), and experiment (3.83 vs. 1.92) aspects compared to strong baselines.
solution: A multi-agent framework called AUTOREPRODUCE employs a collaborative setup between a 'research agent' for text-centric tasks and a 'code agent' for code-oriented tasks, structured into literature review, paper lineage, and code development phases.
A novel 'paper lineage' algorithm addresses implicit knowledge by identifying relevant cited papers and their code repositories, extracting common implementation practices and contextual references.

The code development phase involves iterative refinement, data acquisition with batch sampling for attribute understanding, and method replication with unit testing and debugging to ensure generated code is executable and functionally aligned with the paper.
paper_summary.summary: AUTOREPRODUCE is an automated, end-to-end framework for reproducing AI experiments described in research papers, integrating a novel paper lineage algorithm and iterative debugging to address implicit knowledge and ensure high execution fidelity. It demonstrates superior performance over existing baselines in generating executable code that closely matches the performance of official implementations.

title: AutoReproduce: Automatic AI Experiment Reproduction with Paper Lineage

abstract:
The pace of scientific research, vital for improving human life, is complex,
slow, and needs specialized expertise. Meanwhile, novel, impactful research
often stems from both a deep understanding of prior work, and a
cross-pollination of ideas across domains and fields. To enhance the
productivity of researchers, we propose ResearchAgent, which leverages the
encyclopedic knowledge and linguistic reasoning capabilities of Large Language
Models (LLMs) to assist them in their work. This system automatically defines
novel problems, proposes methods and designs experiments, while iteratively
refining them based on the feedback from collaborative LLM-powered reviewing
agents. Specifically, starting with a core scientific paper, ResearchAgent is
augmented not only with relevant publications by connecting information over an
academic graph but also entities retrieved from a knowledge store derived from
shared underlying concepts mined across numerous papers. Then, mimicking a
scientific approach to improving ideas with peer discussions, we leverage
multiple LLM-based ReviewingAgents that provide reviews and feedback via
iterative revision processes. These reviewing agents are instantiated with
human preference-aligned LLMs whose criteria for evaluation are elicited from
actual human judgments via LLM prompting. We experimentally validate our
ResearchAgent on scientific publications across multiple disciplines, showing
its effectiveness in generating novel, clear, and valid ideas based on both
human and model-based evaluation results. Our initial foray into AI-mediated
scientific research has important implications for the development of future
systems aimed at supporting researchers in their ideation and
operationalization of novel work.

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs), when augmented with broad scientific literature and entity-centric knowledge, can generate comprehensive and open-ended research ideas.
Cross-pollination of concepts, facilitated by retrieving relevant external entities, is critical for introducing novelty and interdisciplinary connections in AI-generated research ideas.

Iterative refinement using LLM-powered agents, guided by evaluation criteria aligned with human scientific judgment, effectively improves the clarity, relevance, and overall quality of generated research proposals.

The effectiveness of AI systems for complex scientific ideation is highly dependent on the capabilities of the underlying LLM, with more powerful models being essential for leveraging augmented knowledge.
originalProblem: Human researchers face challenges in staying abreast of the rapidly expanding scientific literature and synthesizing vast amounts of information to identify novel, impactful research opportunities.
Existing AI applications in scientific discovery primarily focus on experimental validation, leaving the crucial, complex, and open-ended ideation phase largely underexplored by AI.

Traditional hypothesis generation methods often produce localized relationships or sentence-level connections, failing to capture the multi-faceted nature of real-world scientific problems and comprehensive research ideas.
results: ResearchAgent consistently outperformed ablated baselines across all evaluation metrics for problems, methods, and experiment designs in both human and model-based evaluations.
Knowledge augmentation with relevant entities significantly improved the 'Originality' of generated problems and 'Innovativeness' of methods, demonstrating success in cross-pollination of ideas.
The iterative refinement process demonstrably improved the quality of generated ideas, with performance improvements observed up to approximately three iterations.

Human evaluations showed high inter-annotator agreement (e.g., 0.83 Spearman's correlation), and strong human-model agreement (e.g., 0.71 for pairwise comparisons), validating GPT-4 as a reliable evaluation proxy when aligned with human preferences.
solution: An LLM-powered framework that sequentially generates comprehensive research ideas (problem, method, experiment design) by leveraging GPT-4.
Knowledge augmentation strategies include surveying relevant papers via a citation graph and an entity-centric knowledge store that retrieves external entities for cross-disciplinary ideation.

An iterative refinement process uses multiple LLM-powered 'ReviewingAgents' to evaluate generated ideas based on human-aligned criteria, providing feedback for subsequent improvements by the main ResearchAgent.
paper_summary.summary: ResearchAgent introduces an LLM-powered system for iteratively generating comprehensive research ideas, encompassing problem statements, methodologies, and experimental designs, by surveying scientific literature and leveraging an entity-centric knowledge store. The system outperforms baseline methods and enhances idea quality through human-aligned iterative refinement.

title: ResearchAgent: Iterative Research Idea Generation over Scientific  Literature with Large Language Models

abstract:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is beginning to transform the research process by automating the discovery of new solutions. This shift depends on the availability of reliable verifiers, which AI-driven approaches require to validate candidate solutions. Research focused on improving systems performance is especially well-suited to this paradigm because system performance problems naturally admit such verifiers: candidates can be implemented in real systems or simulators and evaluated against predefined workloads. We term this iterative cycle of generation, evaluation, and refinement AI-Driven Research for Systems (ADRS). Using several open-source ADRS instances (i.e., OpenEvolve, GEPA, and ShinkaEvolve), we demonstrate across ten case studies (e.g., multi-region cloud scheduling, mixture-of-experts load balancing, LLM-based SQL, transaction scheduling) that ADRS-generated solutions can match or even outperform human state-of-the-art designs. Based on these findings, we outline best practices (e.g., level of prompt specification, amount of feedback, robust evaluation) for effectively using ADRS, and we discuss future research directions and their implications. Although we do not yet have a universal recipe for applying ADRS across all of systems research, we hope our preliminary findings, together with the challenges we identify, offer meaningful guidance for future work as researcher effort shifts increasingly toward problem formulation and strategic oversight.
Note: This paper is an extension of our prior work [14]. It adds extensive evaluation across multiple ADRS frameworks and provides deeper analysis and insights into best practices.

keyInsights: Systems performance research is particularly amenable to AI-driven discovery due to the availability of reliable and efficient verifiers (simulators), straightforward correctness checks, and manageable code scope for candidate solutions.

LLMs demonstrate a "cross-domain reasoning" capability, synthesizing effective solutions by drawing upon techniques from disparate fields (e.g., political science, economics) that human experts might overlook.

Effective application of ADRS requires adherence to specific best practices for problem specification (e.g., abstraction level, seed diversity), evaluation design (e.g., diverse test sets, precise scoring), and feedback granularity to optimize performance and mitigate issues like reward hacking.
originalProblem: Computer systems performance research has historically relied on human expert intuition and iterative manual design, leading to time-consuming processes and limiting the exploration of vast solution spaces.
A significant portion of research time (over 40%) is spent on manual algorithm discovery, implementation, and initial debugging, hindering the overall pace of innovation.
Identifying novel or superior algorithms for systems optimization is challenging due to the complexity of the problems and potential human cognitive biases.
results: ADRS-generated algorithms matched or outperformed human state-of-the-art solutions in all ten diverse systems case studies, including achieving a 13x speedup for MoE load balancing and 35% greater cost savings for cloud spot instance scheduling.
Most tasks reached near-SOTA performance within a few hours and at a modest cost (typically under $30), demonstrating the practical efficiency and low barrier to entry for the automated approach.

Frameworks exhibited varying success rates (OpenEvolve led in 9 out of 20 cases) and sensitivity to the underlying LLM, with analysis revealing that more modular code, often from GPT-5, was effective for problems requiring the synthesis of multiple techniques.
solution: The paper introduces and evaluates AI-Driven Research for Systems (ADRS), an iterative, automated paradigm that utilizes Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate and refine algorithms for systems problems.

Three open-source ADRS frameworks (OpenEvolve, GEPA, ShinkaEvolve) are empirically assessed using GPT-5 and Gemini-3.0-Pro-Preview across ten diverse case studies in networking, databases, distributed systems, and LLM systems.

The ADRS framework operates as an automated inner loop, managing prompt generation, solution generation, evaluation via simulators, storage, and selection of promising solutions, while a human researcher provides high-level guidance in an outer loop.
paper_summary.summary: The application of AI-Driven Research for Systems (ADRS) frameworks, leveraging Large Language Models, demonstrates their capability to automate and accelerate algorithm discovery for complex systems performance issues. This approach yields AI-generated solutions that match or surpass human state-of-the-art designs, achieving up to 13x speedup or 35% cost savings in empirical evaluations across various domains.

title: Let the Barbarians In: How AI Can Accelerate Systems Performance Research

abstract: Artificial intelligence systems for scientific discovery have demonstrated remarkable potential, yet existing approaches remain largely proprietary and operate in batch-processing modes requiring hours per research cycle, precluding real-time researcher guidance. This paper introduces Deep Research, a multi-agent system enabling interactive scientific investigation with turnaround times measured in minutes. The architecture comprises specialized agents for planning, data analysis, literature search, and novelty detection, unified through a persistent world state that maintains context across iterative research cycles. Two operational modes support different workflows: semi-autonomous mode with selective human checkpoints, and fully autonomous mode for extended investigations. Evaluation on the BixBench computational biology benchmark demonstrated state-of-the-art performance, achieving 48.8% accuracy on open response and 64.4% on multiple-choice evaluation, exceeding existing baselines by 14 to 26 percentage points. Analysis of architectural constraints, including open access literature limitations and challenges inherent to automated novelty assessment, informs practical deployment considerations for AI-assisted scientific workflows.

keyInsights: An interactive, multi-agent architecture maintaining a persistent world state effectively overcomes the limitations of batch-processing AI, enabling true human-AI collaboration and rapid iteration in scientific discovery.
Specialized agents employing iterative code generation and execution loops can achieve state-of-the-art performance on complex, open-ended computational biology analytical tasks.

AI systems for scientific discovery are inherently constrained by factors like reliance on open-access literature, conceptual limitations in novelty assessment, and potential ambiguities within existing benchmarks, emphasizing the need for robust evaluation and human oversight.
originalProblem: Most existing "AI Scientist" systems are proprietary and closed-source, limiting accessibility, reproducibility, and community development.
Current AI-driven scientific platforms largely operate in non-interactive batch-processing modes, requiring long runtimes and precluding real-time researcher guidance and iterative refinement.
The inability to dynamically adjust research directions based on intermediate findings leads to inefficient exploration of alternative hypotheses, often requiring full system reruns.
results: The data analysis agent of "Deep Research" achieved state-of-the-art performance on the BixBench v1.5 computational biology benchmark, scoring 48.8% accuracy in open response, 55.1% in MCQ with refusal, and 64.4% in MCQ without refusal.
The system supports rapid research turnaround times, measured in minutes, facilitating dynamic adjustment of research paths and exploration of hypotheses in real time.
The "BioAgents" orchestrator framework was made publicly available, contributing to increased accessibility and reproducibility in AI-driven scientific discovery.
solution: Developed "Deep Research," a modular, multi-agent AI system built on the "BioAgents" framework, designed to support interactive and iterative scientific workflows using a persistent conversation world state.
Implemented a semi-autonomous "human-in-the-loop" mode with selective checkpoints, alongside a fully autonomous mode, to allow dynamic researcher guidance and control.
Integrated specialized agents for planning, data analysis, literature search, and novelty detection, all operating within a four-stage iterative pipeline for comprehensive research cycles.
paper_summary.summary: Deep Research, an interactive multi-agent AI system, enables accelerated scientific discovery by achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on the BixBench computational biology benchmark for data analysis tasks. The system facilitates real-time collaboration with researchers through rapid, iterative workflows.

title: Rethinking the AI Scientist: Interactive Multi-Agent Workflows for Scientific Discovery

abstract: Discovering scaling laws for predicting model performance at scale is a fundamental and open-ended challenge, mostly reliant on slow, case specific human experimentation. To investigate the potential for LLMs to automate this process, we collect over 5,000 experiments from existing literature and curate eight diverse scaling law discovery tasks. While existing agents struggle to produce accurate law formulas, this paper introduces SLDAgent, an evolution-based agent that co-optimize the scaling law model and the parameters, enabling it to autonomously explore complex relationships between variables. For the first time, we demonstrates that SLDAgent can automatically discover laws that exhibit consistently more accurate extrapolation than their established, human-derived counterparts across all tasks. Through comprehensive analysis, we elucidate why these discovered laws are superior and verify their practical utility in both pretraining and finetuning applications. This work establishes a new paradigm for agentic scientific discovery, showing that AI systems can understand their own scaling behavior, and can contribute novel and practical knowledge back to the research community.

keyInsights: AI agents can achieve 'superhuman' performance in open-ended scientific discovery, consistently outperforming human experts and other AI agents in formulating robust, extrapolative scaling laws.

The co-evolution of both the mathematical expression and its optimization routine is crucial for achieving superior performance, as tailoring the fitting method to the law enhances accuracy and robustness.
SLDAgent often discovers conceptually more principled and dimensionally consistent laws compared to human-derived ones, offering more stable asymptotic behavior and better interpretability.
originalProblem: Discovering neural scaling laws is a manual, intuition-driven, and labor-intensive process, serving as a bottleneck in foundation model development.
Human experts are limited by cognitive biases and prior knowledge, leading to sub-optimal or less generalizable scaling law formulations.
There was a lack of open-ended benchmarks challenging AI agents to autonomously discover novel, extrapolative scientific laws from real-world experimental data.
results: SLDAgent (with GPT-5) achieved an average R² of 0.748 on SLDBench, significantly outperforming the human baseline (0.517) and other state-of-the-art coding agents.
The evolutionary framework consistently improved the performance of various underlying LLMs, boosting R² scores by up to +0.429 compared to their native command-line interfaces.

Discovered laws demonstrated practical utility by analytically deriving near-optimal hyperparameters for pre-training with a 0.067% relative error and achieving 100% relative accuracy in selecting optimal LLMs for fine-tuning.
solution: A comprehensive and open-ended benchmark, SLDBench, was curated from over 5,000 real-world LLM training experiments, emphasizing accurate extrapolation to unseen data.
SLDAgent, a novel evolution-based AI agent, was developed to discover scaling laws by searching over executable Python programs.
The agent features a co-evolutionary design, iteratively optimizing both the symbolic mathematical expression of a scaling law and the parameter-fitting algorithm used for it.
paper_summary.summary: An AI agent designed to discover scaling laws autonomously generated mathematical models that surpass human-derived laws in accuracy and robustness on a new, comprehensive benchmark. This system, called SLDAgent, consistently improved prediction performance for foundation model scaling behaviors and offered practical utility in hyperparameter optimization and model selection.

title: Can Language Models Discover Scaling Laws?

abstract: Rapid development of artificial intelligence has drastically accelerated the development of scientific discovery. Trained with large-scale observation data, deep neural networks extract the underlying patterns in an end-to-end manner and assist human researchers with highly-precised predictions in unseen scenarios. The recent rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) and the empowered autonomous agents enable scientists to gain help through interaction in different stages of their research, including but not limited to literature review, research ideation, idea implementation, and academic writing. However, AI researchers instantiated by foundation model empowered agents with full-process autonomy are still in their infancy. In this paper, we study $\textbf{AI-Generated Science}$ (AIGS), where agents independently and autonomously complete the entire research process and discover scientific laws. By revisiting the definition of scientific research, we argue that $\textit{falsification}$ is the essence of both human research process and the design of an AIGS system. Through the lens of falsification, prior systems attempting towards AI-Generated Science either lack the part in their design, or rely heavily on existing verification engines that narrow the use in specialized domains. In this work, we propose Baby-AIGS as a baby-step demonstration of a full-process AIGS system, which is a multi-agent system with agents in roles representing key research process. By introducing FalsificationAgent, which identify and then verify possible scientific discoveries, we empower the system with explicit falsification. Experiments on three tasks preliminarily show that Baby-AIGS could produce meaningful scientific discoveries, though not on par with experienced human researchers. Finally, we discuss on the limitations of current Baby-AIGS, actionable insights, and related ethical issues in detail.

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating a FALSIFICATIONAGENT based on Karl Popper's philosophy enhances the scientific rigor and trustworthiness of AI-generated discoveries.
A structured DSL is critical for achieving high executability and reliability in AI-driven experimental pipelines, mitigating issues of error-prone natural language interpretation.
Iterative refinement combined with a multi-sampling and re-ranking strategy effectively boosts the creativity and performance improvement capabilities of AI agents in research.
originalProblem: Existing AI-Generated Science (AIGS) systems often lack robust autonomous falsification mechanisms, which is crucial for scientific rigor and trustworthiness.
Prior AIGS efforts struggle with ensuring high executability of proposed methodologies, frequently leading to errors in translating ideas into practical experiments.
AI-driven research processes often generate incremental knowledge or 'tweaks' without a systematic way to explore diverse and impactful ideas efficiently.
results: BABY-AIGS achieved nearly 100% Experiment Success Rate and Overall Success Rate across three machine learning research topics, significantly outperforming AI Scientist (44.8% and 29.2% respectively).

The FALSIFICATIONAGENT successfully produced valid scientific discovery candidates, with qualitative human evaluation showing its potential for rigorous hypothesis testing, though with room for improvement in consistent alignment and correctness compared to human experts.

Demonstrated notable creativity by achieving performance improvements over baseline methods and AI Scientist in tasks like Data Engineering (MT-Bench score 4.77 vs 4.53 baseline, 4.67 AI Scientist for SFT) and Self-Instruct Alignment (3.26 vs 2.45 baseline).
solution: Introduces BABY-AIGS, an LLM-powered multi-agent framework with distinct agents for proposal generation, experimentation, review, and a novel FALSIFICATIONAGENT.
Implements a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) to bridge the gap between abstract research ideas and reliably executable experimental steps, improving execution success rates.
Incorporates an iterative 'Pre-Falsification' phase with a multi-sampling strategy and re-ranking to enhance creativity and explore a broader range of research hypotheses.
paper_summary.summary: The AIGS framework introduces an AI-powered multi-agent system capable of independently conducting scientific research, notably incorporating an autonomous falsification mechanism to systematically test and validate hypotheses. It achieves nearly 100% experimental success rates through a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) and demonstrates performance improvements over baselines in machine learning tasks.

title: AIGS: Generating Science from AI-Powered Automated Falsification

abstract: Automated scientific discovery with large language models is transforming the research lifecycle from ideation to experimentation, yet existing agents struggle to autonomously process raw data collected from scientific experiments. We introduce SciDER, a data-centric end-to-end system that automates the research lifecycle. Unlike traditional frameworks, our specialized agents collaboratively parse and analyze raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in specific data characteristics, and write and execute corresponding code. Evaluation on three benchmarks shows SciDER excels in specialized data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models through its self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop. Distributed as a modular Python package, we also provide easy-to-use PyPI packages with a lightweight web interface to accelerate autonomous, data-driven research and aim to be accessible to all researchers and developers.

keyInsights: Autonomous processing of raw experimental data, rather than curated datasets, is crucial for bridging the gap between AI-driven research and practical scientific applications.
A self-evolving memory system that categorizes and retrieves relevant knowledge allows AI agents to achieve continuous learning and enhanced proficiency in both general and domain-specific research.

Integrating specialized agents within a comprehensive end-to-end pipeline, along with iterative feedback, enables AI to effectively cover the entire scientific research lifecycle and adapt to diverse scientific domains.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based research agents struggle to autonomously parse, clean, and analyze diverse, raw experimental data, limiting their application in real-world scientific investigations.

General-purpose AI assistants often lack the domain-specific adaptability to translate abstract scientific ideas into precise experimental designs and executable code for specialized scientific fields.
Current automated research frameworks often provide only partial automation, lacking integration across the entire scientific lifecycle from ideation to iterative refinement and result analysis.
results: SciDER achieved a novelty score of 47.06 on AI-Idea-Bench, nearly doubling that of state-of-the-art frameworks (e.g., AI-Scientist's 25.03), demonstrating superior ability to generate original research proposals.
It attained a 36.40% gold medal rate (pass@1) on the MLE-Bench, outperforming specialized ML agents like AIRA by 7.76% in machine learning experimentation.
SciDER surpassed GPT-5 on the SciCode benchmark for domain-specific coding, achieving a 15.38% solve rate for main problems (vs. GPT-5's 13.85%) and 42.71% for sub-problems (vs. GPT-5's 38.26%).
solution: SciDER introduces a data-centric, end-to-end system architecture with specialized LLM-based agents for Ideation, Data Analysis, Experimentation (Coding & Execution), and Critics & Feedback.

It implements a self-evolving memory mechanism, categorized into short-term, task-specific long-term, and project-specific long-term, to enable continuous test-time learning and adapt to new knowledge.

The system utilizes an iterative research framework with a critic-led feedback loop that evaluates outputs at each stage, driving self-refinement and ensuring high-quality research proposals and executable code.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from William & Mary, University of Minnesota, and UNC Chapel Hill developed SciDER, an end-to-end AI agent for scientific discovery that autonomously handles raw experimental data, generates hypotheses, designs experiments, and analyzes results. The system achieves a 47.06 novelty score on AI-Idea-Bench, a 36.40% gold medal rate on MLE-Bench, and outperforms GPT-5 on domain-specific coding tasks in the SciCode benchmark.

title: SciDER: Scientific Data-centric End-to-end Researcher

abstract: Current Autonomous Scientific Research (ASR) systems, despite leveraging large language models (LLMs) and agentic architectures, remain constrained by fixed workflows and toolsets that prevent adaptation to evolving tasks and environments. We introduce Mimosa, an evolving multi-agent framework that automatically synthesizes task-specific multi-agent workflows and iteratively refines them through experimental feedback. Mimosa leverages the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for dynamic tool discovery, generates workflow topologies via a meta-orchestrator, executes subtasks through code-generating agents that invoke available tools and scientific software libraries, and scores executions with an LLM-based judge whose feedback drives workflow refinement. On ScienceAgentBench, Mimosa achieves a success rate of 43.1% with DeepSeek-V3.2, surpassing both single-agent baselines and static multi-agent configurations. Our results further reveal that models respond heterogeneously to multi-agent decomposition and iterative learning, indicating that the benefits of workflow evolution depend on the capabilities of the underlying execution model. Beyond these benchmarks, Mimosa modular architecture and tool-agnostic design make it readily extensible, and its fully logged execution traces and archived workflows support auditability by preserving every analytical step for inspection and potential replication. Combined with domain-expert guidance, the framework has the potential to automate a broad range of computationally accessible scientific tasks across disciplines. Released as a fully open-source platform, Mimosa aims to provide an open foundation for community-driven ASR.

keyInsights: Dynamically evolving multi-agent workflow topologies significantly enhance the adaptability and performance of autonomous scientific research systems.

Decomposing complex scientific problems into subtasks handled by specialized agents within a flexible DAG structure helps mitigate issues like semantic drift and context limitations inherent in long-horizon LLM executions.

The effectiveness of multi-agent and iterative learning approaches varies heterogeneously across different underlying language models, suggesting that optimal ASR architectures might need tailoring to specific model capabilities.
originalProblem: The scientific community faces a data-to-knowledge bottleneck and a reproducibility crisis due to fragmented methods and reporting.
Existing LLM-based AI-agent systems are limited by context constraints, information loss, semantic drift, and architectural rigidity over long-horizon tasks.
Many multi-agent systems rely on static coordination protocols, hindering their adaptation to evolving experimental demands or unexpected results.
results: DeepSeek-V3.2, when integrated with Mimosa's iterative-learning multi-agent configuration, achieved the highest success rate of 43.1% on the ScienceAgentBench benchmark.
Iterative refinement consistently improved performance for up to 8 iterations, with DeepSeek-V3.2 showing a 34% relative success rate increase from one-shot to iterative learning.
The framework enabled DeepSeek-V3.2 to achieve its highest success rate at an average cost of $1.7 per task, demonstrating a pathway towards cost-efficient scientific AI.
solution: Mimosa introduces an evolving multi-agent framework that treats workflow topology, represented as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), as a mutable and learnable component.
A meta-orchestrator dynamically generates initial workflows (via case-based retrieval or de novo synthesis) and iteratively refines them based on structured feedback from an LLM-as-a-judge.
The framework incorporates dynamic tool discovery and integration using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Toolomics, allowing agents to access and utilize distributed scientific tools.
paper_summary.summary: Mimosa, a multi-agent framework for scientific research, dynamically generates and iteratively refines workflow topologies to enhance adaptability and performance for computational scientific tasks. The framework achieved a 43.1% success rate with DeepSeek-V3.2 on the ScienceAgentBench benchmark, demonstrating improved capabilities over static agent systems.

title: Mimosa Framework: Toward Evolving Multi-Agent Systems for Scientific Research

abstract: The exponential growth of scientific literature poses unprecedented challenges for researchers attempting to synthesize knowledge across rapidly evolving fields. We present \textbf{Agentic AutoSurvey}, a multi-agent framework for automated survey generation that addresses fundamental limitations in existing approaches. Our system employs four specialized agents (Paper Search Specialist, Topic Mining \& Clustering, Academic Survey Writer, and Quality Evaluator) working in concert to generate comprehensive literature surveys with superior synthesis quality. Through experiments on six representative LLM research topics from COLM 2024 categories, we demonstrate that our multi-agent approach achieves significant improvements over existing baselines, scoring 8.18/10 compared to AutoSurvey's 4.77/10. The multi-agent architecture processes 75--443 papers per topic (847 total across six topics) while targeting high citation coverage (often $\geq$80\% on 75--100-paper sets; lower on very large sets such as RLHF) through specialized agent orchestration. Our 12-dimension evaluation captures organization, synthesis integration, and critical analysis beyond basic metrics. These findings demonstrate that multi-agent architectures represent a meaningful advancement for automated literature survey generation in rapidly evolving scientific domains.

keyInsights: Specialized multi-agent orchestration significantly improves the quality of automated academic surveys across all dimensions, including core quality, writing quality, and content depth.
Automated systems can achieve sophisticated thematic integration, identify emergent patterns, and pinpoint research gaps, moving beyond simple paper enumeration.
The comprehensive 12-dimensional evaluation framework provides a robust and nuanced standard for assessing the quality of AI-generated academic content, pushing towards more rigorous assessment.
originalProblem: The exponential growth of scientific literature, especially in rapidly evolving fields like LLMs, creates overwhelming challenges for researchers to manually synthesize knowledge.
Existing automated survey generation tools produce inadequate synthesis quality, offer limited citation coverage (typically 60-70%), and rely on simplistic evaluation frameworks.
Researchers struggle to keep pace with new developments, identify emerging patterns, and conduct comprehensive literature reviews due to the sheer volume of new publications.
results: Agentic AutoSurvey achieved an average quality score of 8.18/10, representing a 71% improvement over the AutoSurvey baseline's score of 4.77/10.
The system demonstrated high efficiency, processing collections of 75 to 443 papers per topic (847 total) in an impressive 15-20 minutes.

It showed a 99% improvement in 'Core Quality' (citation coverage, accuracy, synthesis) and a 68% improvement in 'Writing Quality' compared to the baseline, effectively organizing papers into meaningful thematic clusters.
solution: A modular, multi-agent framework was developed, featuring four specialized agents: Paper Search Specialist, Topic Mining & Clustering, Academic Survey Writer, and Quality Evaluator.
The system employs advanced search strategies, semantic embeddings for optimal K-means clustering, and a synthesis-driven approach for content generation.
A comprehensive 12-dimensional evaluation framework, assessed by an 'agent-as-judge', was established to provide nuanced quality assessment for generated surveys.
paper_summary.summary: Agentic AutoSurvey presents a multi-agent framework that uses specialized large language models to automate the generation of academic literature surveys. The system achieved an average quality score of 8.18/10, marking a 71% improvement over the AutoSurvey baseline, while efficiently processing hundreds of papers in 15-20 minutes with enhanced synthesis quality and coverage for rapidly expanding scientific fields.

title: Agentic AutoSurvey: Let LLMs Survey LLMs

abstract: Automated paper reproduction has emerged as a promising approach to accelerate scientific research, employing multi-step workflow frameworks to systematically convert academic papers into executable code. However, existing frameworks often lack mechanisms to verify and refine the outputs at each generation step, or rely heavily on manually designed prompts for self-refinement, which limits their adaptability and scalability. To address these limitations, we propose a prompt-free collaborative agent framework that automatically enhances the quality of paper-to-code generation. Our approach employs two collaborative agents: a verification agent that examines whether the outputs at each step satisfy the requirements specified in the corresponding system prompt, and a refinement agent that revises the outputs based on the identified issues. Unlike previous methods that require human experts to craft specific refinement prompts for each step, our framework achieves automatic verification and improvement by leveraging only the original system prompts. We integrate our collaborative agents into the Paper2Code framework and conduct comprehensive experiments on PaperBench Code-Dev and Paper2CodeBench datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly improves the accuracy and completeness of reproduced code, achieving performance gains of approximately 15\% and 13\%, respectively, compared to the baseline without our agents. Furthermore, comparative experiments against Self-Refine validate the robustness and consistency of our prompt-free approach across different datasets.
title: Enhancing Automated Paper Reproduction via Prompt-Free Collaborative Agents

abstract: Existing automated research systems operate as stateless, linear pipelines -- generating outputs without maintaining any persistent understanding of the research landscape they navigate. They process papers sequentially, propose ideas without structured gap analysis, and lack mechanisms for agents to verify, challenge, or refine each other's findings. We present \textbf{AI-Supervisor}, a multi-agent orchestration framework where specialized agents provide end-to-end AI research supervision driven by human interests -- from literature review through gap discovery, method development, evaluation, and paper writing -- through autonomous exploration and self-correcting updates of research knowledge. Unlike sequential pipelines, AI-Supervisor maintains a continuously evolving \emph{Research World Model}, implemented as a Knowledge Graph, that captures methods, benchmarks, known limitations, and unexplored gaps, serving as shared memory across all agents and enabling agents to explore and build upon a structured understanding of the research landscape. The framework introduces three architectural contributions: (1) \emph{structured gap discovery} that decomposes methods into core modules, validates their performance across benchmarks, and maps the specific gaps each module creates; (2) \emph{self-correcting discovery loops} that probe why modules succeed on certain problems and fail on others, whether benchmarks carry hidden biases, and whether evaluation protocols remain adequate for emerging challenges; and (3) \emph{self-improving development loops} governed by cross-domain mechanism search that iteratively targets failing modules by finding solutions from other scientific fields. All agents operate under a \emph{consensus mechanism} where independent findings are corroborated before being committed to the Research World Model.
title: AI-Supervisor: Autonomous AI Research Supervision via a Persistent Research World Model

abstract:
In this paper we introduce ResearchCodeAgent, a novel multi-agent system
leveraging large language models (LLMs) agents to automate the codification of
research methodologies described in machine learning literature. The system
bridges the gap between high-level research concepts and their practical
implementation, allowing researchers auto-generating code of existing research
papers for benchmarking or building on top-of existing methods specified in the
literature with availability of partial or complete starter code.
ResearchCodeAgent employs a flexible agent architecture with a comprehensive
action suite, enabling context-aware interactions with the research
environment. The system incorporates a dynamic planning mechanism, utilizing
both short and long-term memory to adapt its approach iteratively. We evaluate
ResearchCodeAgent on three distinct machine learning tasks with distinct task
complexity and representing different parts of the ML pipeline: data
augmentation, optimization, and data batching. Our results demonstrate the
system's effectiveness and generalizability, with 46.9% of generated code being
high-quality and error-free, and 25% showing performance improvements over
baseline implementations. Empirical analysis shows an average reduction of
57.9% in coding time compared to manual implementation. We observe higher gains
for more complex tasks. ResearchCodeAgent represents a significant step towards
automating the research implementation process, potentially accelerating the
pace of machine learning research.

keyInsights: An adaptive, multi-agent LLM system with dynamic planning significantly outperforms rigid or one-shot approaches in converting abstract research methodologies into executable code.
Integrating short-term and long-term memory, along with a tiered LLM cascade, enhances the agent's robustness and problem-solving capability in complex coding tasks.
The iterative refinement process, mimicking human research workflow, enables the system to achieve high code quality and substantial time savings, especially for more complex implementations.
originalProblem: The rapid pace of ML research creates a bottleneck where researchers spend significant time translating abstract methodological descriptions from papers into functional, executable code.

Existing LLM-based code generation tools often focus on generating code from scratch or for highly curated tasks, not on refining or extending existing repository-level starter code based on complex research papers.
Manual implementation diverts valuable time from higher-level intellectual tasks like ideation, exploration, and novel experimental design.
results: ResearchCodeAgent produced high-quality (S1) code in 46.88% of runs, significantly outperforming a Prescribed Plan (0%) and Single LLM Call (37.5%) baselines.
The system reduced erroneous outputs (Category C) to 43.75%, compared to 62.5% for Prescribed Plan and 81.25% for Single LLM Call.
ResearchCodeAgent achieved an average time saving of 57.86% compared to manual implementation, with its strongest gains (up to 77.82%) observed in high-complexity tasks like FLAG.
solution: ResearchCodeAgent (RCA) is introduced as a multi-agent LLM system designed to automate the codification of ML research methodologies from abstract paper descriptions into executable code.

RCA integrates with an iterative 'research environment' by taking inputs like methodology descriptions, pseudocode, and existing starter code, then refining it through a suite of programmatic and LLM-driven actions.

The system employs dynamic planning with short-term and long-term memory, an LLM cascade (Gemini 1.5 Flash, Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4), and programmatic constructs to enhance robustness and prevent common LLM agent failures.
paper_summary.summary: ResearchCodeAgent, developed by TCS Research, is an LLM multi-agent system that automates the translation of abstract machine learning research methodologies into executable code, particularly by refining existing starter code. The system generated high-quality code in nearly half of its trials and achieved an average time saving of 57.86% compared to manual implementation.

title: ResearchCodeAgent: An LLM Multi-Agent System for Automated Codification  of Research Methodologies

abstract: As AI agents evolve, the community is rapidly shifting from single Large Language Models (LLMs) to Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) to overcome cognitive bottlenecks in automated research. However, the optimal multi-agent coordination framework for these autonomous agents remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we present a systematic empirical study investigating the comparative efficacy of distinct multi-agent structures for automated machine learning optimization. Utilizing a rigorously controlled, execution-based testbed equipped with Git worktree isolation and explicit global memory, we benchmark a single-agent baseline against two multi-agent paradigms: a subagent architecture (parallel exploration with post-hoc consolidation) and an agent team architecture (experts with pre-execution handoffs). By evaluating these systems under strictly fixed computational time budgets, our findings reveal a fundamental trade-off between operational stability and theoretical deliberation. The subagent mode functions as a highly resilient, high-throughput search engine optimal for broad, shallow optimizations under strict time constraints. Conversely, the agent team topology exhibits higher operational fragility due to multi-author code generation but achieves the deep theoretical alignment necessary for complex architectural refactoring given extended compute budgets. These empirical insights provide actionable guidelines for designing future autoresearch systems, advocating for dynamically routed architectures that adapt their collaborative structures to real-time task complexity.

keyInsights: A fundamental trade-off exists between operational stability and the depth of theoretical deliberation in multi-agent architectures; subagents provide rapid, shallow optimization while agent teams facilitate complex, architectural refactoring.

Effective multi-agent collaboration can be achieved through distinct coordination mechanisms: parallel exploration with post-hoc consolidation for broad searches, and sequential expert deliberation for integrated, complex modifications.
Rigorous testbed design, including isolated environments and structured interactions, is crucial for mitigating confounding variables and enabling reliable empirical comparisons of agentic systems.
originalProblem: Single-agent Large Language Models (LLMs) face limitations in automated research, including maintaining long-horizon context, hallucinating during code refactoring, and getting stuck in deterministic search paths.
There is a lack of systematic empirical studies comparing optimal multi-agent coordination frameworks for autonomous agents in dynamic, empirical machine learning optimization.
Current evaluations of autonomous code generation agents often suffer from environmental contamination and context degradation, hindering fair comparisons of multi-agent topologies.
results: Subagent architectures demonstrated a distinct early-stage advantage under the 300s budget, achieving seven effective improvements over 50 rounds by rapidly accumulating localized optimizations through parallel exploration and successful patch merging.

Agent teams, while showing slower initial progress and higher operational fragility, generated modifications with significantly higher diversity and structural complexity, enabling coupled architectural changes across multiple dimensions (e.g., attention patterns, learning schedules, embedding sizes).

The subagent mode proved highly stable and efficient as an empirical search engine, maintaining the lowest effective preflight failure and training crash rates due to isolated worktrees, whereas agent teams experienced higher failure rates attributed to the fragility of multi-author code generation via sequential text handoffs.
solution: The research formulated automated research as a constrained search optimization problem, aiming to minimize validation bits per byte (val_bpb) within fixed computational budgets (300s, 600s).
A rigorous, reproducible evaluation testbed was developed, featuring Git worktree isolation, structured patch contracts, preflight validation, and explicit global memory to ensure fair comparisons.

Three architectures were compared: a single-agent baseline, a hierarchical subagent model for parallel exploration and post-hoc consolidation, and a collaborative agent team model for pre-execution deliberation and sequential handoffs.
paper_summary.summary: An empirical study investigated how different multi-agent coordination frameworks affect automated machine learning research efficiency. It found that subagent architectures excel in rapid, shallow optimization with high stability, while agent teams, despite higher fragility, facilitate deeper, more complex architectural refactoring and diverse modifications.

title: An Empirical Study of Multi-Agent Collaboration for Automated Research

abstract: Recent advances in AI agents for software engineering and scientific discovery have demonstrated remarkable capabilities, yet their application to developing novel ranking models in commercial search engines remains unexplored. In this paper, we present an AI Co-Scientist framework that automates the full search ranking research pipeline: from idea generation to code implementation and GPU training job scheduling with expert in the loop. Our approach strategically employs single-LLM agents for routine tasks while leveraging multi-LLM consensus agents (GPT 5.2, Gemini Pro 3, and Claude Opus 4.5) for challenging phases such as results analysis and idea generation. To our knowledge, this is the first study in the ranking community to utilize an AI Co-Scientist framework for algorithmic research. We demonstrate that this framework discovered a novel technique for handling sequence features, with all model enhancements produced automatically, yielding substantial offline performance improvements. Our findings suggest that AI systems can discover ranking architectures comparable to those developed by human experts while significantly reducing routine research workloads.
title: AI Co-Scientist for Ranking: Discovering Novel Search Ranking Models alongside LLM-based AI Agents with Cloud Computing Access

abstract:
Conventional biomedical research is increasingly labor-intensive due to the
exponential growth of scientific literature and datasets. Artificial
intelligence (AI), particularly Large Language Models (LLMs), has the potential
to revolutionize this process by automating various steps. Still, significant
challenges remain, including the need for multidisciplinary expertise,
logicality of experimental design, and performance measurements. This paper
introduces BioResearcher, the first end-to-end automated system designed to
streamline the entire biomedical research process involving dry lab
experiments. BioResearcher employs a modular multi-agent architecture,
integrating specialized agents for search, literature processing, experimental
design, and programming. By decomposing complex tasks into logically related
sub-tasks and utilizing a hierarchical learning approach, BioResearcher
effectively addresses the challenges of multidisciplinary requirements and
logical complexity. Furthermore, BioResearcher incorporates an LLM-based
reviewer for in-process quality control and introduces novel evaluation metrics
to assess the quality and automation of experimental protocols. BioResearcher
successfully achieves an average execution success rate of 63.07% across eight
previously unmet research objectives. The generated protocols, on average,
outperform typical agent systems by 22.0% on five quality metrics. The system
demonstrates significant potential to reduce researchers' workloads and
accelerate biomedical discoveries, paving the way for future innovations in
automated research systems.

organization_info.name: National Institute for Data Science in Health and Medicine

keyInsights: A modular multi-agent LLM architecture can effectively integrate diverse domain expertise (e.g., biology, programming) required for complex scientific research tasks.

Hierarchical processing and iterative quality control via LLM-based reviewers significantly enhance the logical soundness, detail, and correctness of generated scientific reports and experimental protocols.
LLM-generated queries and filtering mechanisms can be highly effective in curating relevant scientific literature and datasets, often outperforming manual methods.
originalProblem: Biomedical research is increasingly labor-intensive due to the exponential growth of scientific literature and data, making it challenging for researchers to stay updated.
Existing AI tools enhance specific steps but lack end-to-end automation for the entire dry lab biomedical research process, which demands multidisciplinary expertise and complex logical reasoning.
There is a lack of robust, automated, and fine-grained evaluation metrics for end-to-end AI-driven research outputs in the biomedical domain.
results: BioResearcher achieved an average execution success rate of 63.07% for generated dry lab protocols and code, while baseline LLM agent systems yielded near-zero success rates.
Generated protocols demonstrated superior quality, with 62.7% higher completeness, 30.0% more detail, and 84.9% greater correctness compared to the best baselines.

The LLM-based query generator and filter agents showed high effectiveness, with the filter achieving an 84% accuracy in classifying useful papers/datasets, and LLM reviewers improved overall performance (e.g., 0.045 increase in Correctness).
solution: BioResearcher, an end-to-end automated system, utilizes a modular multi-agent framework powered by GPT-4o, with specialized agents for search, literature processing, experimental design, and programming.
It employs a hierarchical learning approach for literature comprehension and experimental protocol generation, decomposing complex problems into manageable sub-tasks.
The system incorporates LLM-based reviewers for continuous quality control and iterative refinement throughout the research pipeline, ensuring reliability and accuracy.
paper_summary.summary: BioResearcher introduces an end-to-end automated system for dry lab biomedical research, leveraging a multi-agent Large Language Model framework to streamline the process from research objective to executable code. The system achieved a 63.07% execution success rate for generated protocols and code, significantly outperforming existing LLM-based agent systems in protocol quality, completeness, and correctness.

title: From Intention To Implementation: Automating Biomedical Research via  LLMs

abstract: AutoModel is an agent based architecture for the full lifecycle of industrial recommender systems. Instead of a fixed recall and ranking pipeline, AutoModel organizes recommendation as a set of interacting evolution agents with long term memory and self improvement capability. We instantiate three core agents along the axes of models, features, and resources: AutoTrain for model design and training, AutoFeature for data analysis and feature evolution, and AutoPerf for performance, deployment, and online experimentation. A shared coordination and knowledge layer connects these agents and records decisions, configurations, and outcomes. Through a case study of a module called paper autotrain, we show how AutoTrain automates paper driven model reproduction by closing the loop from method parsing to code generation, large scale training, and offline comparison, reducing manual effort for method transfer. AutoModel enables locally automated yet globally aligned evolution of large scale recommender systems and can be generalized to other AI systems such as search and advertising.

keyInsights: Recommender system components and lifecycle stages can be reframed as independently evaluable and continuously evolvable agents, moving beyond static, human-intensive pipelines.
Closed-loop perception-decision-execution-feedback mechanisms embedded within specialized agents enable autonomous and continuous adaptation of models and features.
A unified knowledge layer and explicit coordination layer are crucial for integrating agent activities, storing shared experiences, and ensuring global alignment of local optimizations.
originalProblem: Traditional industrial recommender systems suffer from structural rigidity and fixed modules, requiring manual intervention for improvements.
The human-centric iteration paradigm for model evolution is slow, brittle, and lacks a unified representation of lifecycle state and knowledge.
Localized optimizations by isolated automation tools often fail to align with global business objectives due to fragmented knowledge and uncoordinated processes.
results: The `paper_auto_train` case study successfully demonstrated the autonomous reproduction and adaptation of a research paper ('Gated Attention for Large Language Models').
The system automated paper discovery, method abstraction into structured descriptions, code planning, and implementation (including generating specific code modifications to existing modules).

Automated submission and tracking of training jobs for both baseline and experimental variants were achieved, showcasing the capability to manage parallel experimentation and log detailed execution traces.
solution: Introduction of AutoModel, an agentic architecture for the full recommender model lifecycle, structured into Decision, Evolution, and Infrastructure layers.

The core Evolution layer comprises three specialized agents: AutoFeature (manages data/feature evolution), AutoTrain (evolves model architectures/training procedures), and AutoPerf (optimizes resource, risk, and deployment).

A Coordination Layer orchestrates cross-agent workflows to achieve long-term goals, while a Knowledge Layer provides shared memory and experience, recording all problem definitions, configurations, and experimental outcomes.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group developed AutoModel, an agentic architecture for managing the full lifecycle of industrial recommender systems. This system replaces rigid pipelines with a framework for continuous, autonomous evolution, demonstrated by its `paper_auto_train` component which automates research paper reproduction, including method abstraction, code generation, and training job management.

title: AgenticRS-Architecture: System Design for Agentic Recommender Systems

abstract: Existing alignment methods for preference optimization of large language models (LLMs) aim to enhance model performance by utilizing pairs of positive and negative samples. However, due to the limited capacity of models in scoring or generating responses, the quality of positive and negative samples may become similar during training, which complicates optimization for preference learning. To address this issue, we introduce SeaPO, a Strategic Error Amplification method that leverages three error types commonly occurring in LLMs to introduce specific error patterns into the model Preference Optimization. This strategy ensures that negative samples are more erroneous than positive samples and preference-based training is employed to mitigate the occurrence of these errors, thereby enhancing model performance. Evaluations across five capability dimensions and different model scales (1.5B to 14B) demonstrate that the generated data significantly improved overall model performance, particularly in terms of truthfulness, with improvements of 5-10 percentage points observed. Further analysis reveals that task performance varies depending on the error types introduced. Injecting the most common error types improves performance in related tasks, while a mix of error types leads to a broader performance enhancement: most tasks show stable improvements, while a few tasks exhibit significant gains.

organization_info.name: Huawei Cloud Computing Technologies Co., Ltd.

title: SeaPO: Strategic Error Amplification for Robust Preference Optimization of Large Language Models

abstract: Automating scientific discovery in complex, experiment-driven domains requires more than iterative mutation of programs; it demands structured hypothesis management, environment interaction, and principled reflection. We present OR-Agent, a configurable multi-agent research framework designed for automated exploration in rich experimental environments. OR-Agent organizes research as a structured tree-based workflow that explicitly models branching hypothesis generation and systematic backtracking, enabling controlled management of research trajectories beyond simple mutation-crossover loops. At its core, we introduce an evolutionary-systematic ideation mechanism that unifies evolutionary selection of research starting points, comprehensive research plan generation, and coordinated exploration within a research tree. We introduce a hierarchical optimization-inspired reflection system in which short-term reflections act as verbal gradients, long-term reflections as verbal momentum, and memory compression as semantic weight decay, collectively forming a principled mechanism for governing research dynamics. We conduct extensive experiments across classical combinatorial optimization benchmarks as well as simulation-based cooperative driving scenarios. Results demonstrate that OR-Agent outperforms strong evolutionary baselines while providing a general, extensible, and inspectable framework for AI-assisted scientific discovery. All code and experimental data are publicly available at this https URL.
title: OR-Agent: Bridging Evolutionary Search and Structured Research for Automated Algorithm Discovery

abstract: Multi-agent systems powered by large language models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse domains, yet existing automated design approaches seek monolithic solutions that fail to adapt resource allocation based on query complexity and domain requirements. This paper introduces AutoMaAS, a self-evolving multi-agent architecture search framework that leverages neural architecture search principles to automatically discover optimal agent configurations through dynamic operator lifecycle management and automated machine learning techniques. Our approach incorporates four key innovations: (1) automatic operator generation, fusion, and elimination based on performance-cost analysis, (2) dynamic cost-aware optimization with real-time parameter adjustment, (3) online feedback integration for continuous architecture refinement, and (4) enhanced interpretability through decision tracing mechanisms. Extensive experiments across six benchmarks demonstrate that AutoMaAS achieves 1.0-7.1\% performance improvement while reducing inference costs by 3-5\% compared to state-of-the-art methods. The framework shows superior transferability across datasets and LLM backbones, establishing a new paradigm for automated multi-agent system design in the era of large language models.

keyInsights: Dynamic adaptation of multi-agent LLM architectures, tailored per query, significantly enhances performance and resource efficiency over static designs.
A self-evolving operator pool, capable of generating, fusing, and eliminating operators based on their health and utility, is critical for maintaining system adaptability in dynamic environments.
Integrating dynamic, multi-objective cost optimization with real-time online feedback mechanisms is essential for deploying multi-agent systems that are both high-performing and economically viable.
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent LLM architectures are often static and 'one-size-fits-all,' leading to inefficient resource allocation for diverse task complexities.
Current frameworks rely on predefined, fixed operator pools, which limits their adaptability to evolving domains and new functionalities, necessitating manual updates.
Optimization typically prioritizes accuracy with static cost constraints, failing to account for dynamic real-world cost factors and multi-dimensional cost considerations.
results: AutoMaAS achieved consistent accuracy improvements of 1.0% to 7.1% across six diverse benchmarks, including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and tool usage, outperforming 14 baseline methods.
The framework reduced total inference costs by 3-5% compared to state-of-the-art methods, reaching an average cost of 58% relative to the CoT baseline, while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of Dynamic Lifecycle Management (3.1% accuracy gain) and Multi-Objective Cost Optimization (1.8% accuracy gain, 17 percentage point relative cost reduction), validating each component's contribution.

The system successfully generated 12 fused operators (e.g., 'CoT + Self-Refine' reduced token consumption by 18% and improved accuracy by 4.2%) and eliminated 8 inefficient ones, demonstrating effective self-evolution and improved efficiency.
solution: An 'Agentic Supernet' with a Query-Dependent Controller samples architectures dynamically, optimizing a conditional distribution of multi-agent configurations based on incoming queries.
Dynamic Operator Lifecycle Management allows for the automatic generation, fusion, and elimination of operators based on performance and utility, ensuring continuous capability evolution.

A Multi-Objective Dynamic Cost Optimization framework incorporates a cost tensor with adaptive weights for dimensions like token usage, API calls, and latency, adjusting to real-time conditions such as system load and API pricing.

Online Feedback Integration uses explicit user ratings, implicit behavioral signals, and internal system metrics to continuously refine architecture selection, complemented by interpretability features like decision tracing and counterfactual analysis.
paper_summary.summary: Peking University researchers present AutoMaAS, a framework designed for self-evolving multi-agent architecture search within Large Language Models. This system dynamically adapts architectures, manages operator lifecycles, and optimizes for multi-objective costs, demonstrating improved performance and cost efficiency across various benchmarks.

title: AutoMaAS: Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Architecture Search for Large Language Models

abstract: Recent LLM-based agents have demonstrated strong capabilities in automated ML engineering. However, they heavily rely on repeated full training runs to evaluate candidate solutions, resulting in significant computational overhead, limited scalability to large search spaces, and slow iteration cycles. To address these challenges, we introduce ArchPilot, a multi-agent system that integrates architecture generation, proxy-based evaluation, and adaptive search into a unified framework. ArchPilot consists of three specialized agents: an orchestration agent that coordinates the search process using a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)-inspired novel algorithm with a restart mechanism and manages memory of previous candidates; a generation agent that iteratively generates, improves, and debugs candidate architectures; and an evaluation agent that executes proxy training runs, generates and optimizes proxy functions, and aggregates the proxy scores into a fidelity-aware performance metric. This multi-agent collaboration allows ArchPilot to prioritize high-potential candidates with minimal reliance on expensive full training runs, facilitating efficient ML engineering under limited budgets. Experiments on MLE-Bench demonstrate that ArchPilot outperforms SOTA baselines such as AIDE and ML-Master, validating the effectiveness of our multi-agent system.

keyInsights: Decoupling ML engineering tasks into specialized, collaborating agents significantly enhances efficiency, scalability, and clarity of responsibility for LLM-driven systems.
Adaptive, weighted aggregation of zero-cost proxies, dynamically optimized through ridge-regularized least-squares, provides a reliable and cost-effective method for guiding the search process.

Integrating a tree restart mechanism within an MCTS-inspired search allows the system to adapt its exploration strategy to evolving proxy reliability, ensuring consistent guidance with up-to-date evaluation metrics.
originalProblem: LLM-based ML engineering agents incur prohibitive computational costs due to their reliance on repeated full training runs for evaluating candidate solutions.
This limitation restricts their scalability to larger search spaces and results in slow iteration cycles, hindering practical application under realistic compute budgets.
Existing LLM agents often lack modularity, integrating all functionalities within a monolithic loop, which prevents independent optimization and efficient resource management.
results: ArchPilot achieved superior overall performance on the MLE-Bench benchmark, demonstrating a higher Valid Submission Rate (0.893) and a better Mean Normalized Ranking (0.6149) compared to AIDE and ML-Master.

The system showed pronounced performance gains on high-difficulty tasks, where its Mean Normalized Ranking of 0.6496 significantly surpassed competitors, highlighting its efficiency in complex scenarios.
ArchPilot maintained its performance lead across a range of computational budgets (1 to 6 GPU-hours), indicating consistent efficiency and effective resource utilization even under tight constraints.
solution: ArchPilot introduces a modular multi-agent architecture, separating responsibilities into an Orchestration Agent (OA), Generation Agent (GA), and Evaluation Agent (EA).
It implements an adaptive, proxy-based evaluation pipeline where the EA uses multiple cheap proxies to estimate model performance, reducing the need for costly full training runs.

The OA employs an MCTS-inspired search algorithm with a novel tree restart mechanism that dynamically recomputes aggregated proxy scores and reinitializes the search tree when proxy weights are updated.
paper_summary.summary: ArchPilot is a proxy-guided, multi-agent system designed for machine learning engineering that automates ML pipeline design with enhanced efficiency. It leverages specialized agents and adaptive proxy evaluations to reduce computational costs while achieving superior solution quality across diverse tasks.

title: ArchPilot: A Proxy-Guided Multi-Agent Approach for Machine Learning Engineering

abstract: We present \textbf{Deep Researcher Agent}, an open-source framework that enables large language model (LLM) agents to autonomously conduct deep learning experiments around the clock. Unlike existing AI research assistants that focus on paper writing or code generation, our system addresses the full experiment lifecycle: hypothesis formation, code implementation, training execution, result analysis, and iterative refinement. The framework introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{Zero-Cost Monitoring} -- a monitoring paradigm that incurs zero LLM API costs during model training by relying solely on process-level checks and log file reads; (2) \textbf{Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory} -- a memory architecture capped at $\sim$5K characters regardless of runtime duration, preventing the unbounded context growth that plagues long-running agents; and (3) \textbf{Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker Architecture} -- a multi-agent design where each worker agent is equipped with only 3--5 tools, reducing per-call token overhead by up to 73\%. In sustained deployments spanning 30+ days, the framework autonomously completed 500+ experiment cycles across four concurrent research projects, achieving a 52\% improvement over baseline metrics in one project through 200+ automated experiments -- all at an average LLM cost of \$0.08 per 24-hour cycle. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLMs offer minimal utility during the active training phase of deep learning experiments; thus, monitoring can be offloaded to local OS-level checks without LLM interaction, significantly reducing costs.

Maintaining a constant-size, two-tier memory system (project brief and rolling memory log) is critical for the long-term stability, performance, and cost-efficiency of autonomous agents by preventing context window overflows.

Employing a leader-worker multi-agent architecture with specialized worker agents, each equipped with only a minimal set of necessary tools, drastically reduces LLM token overhead and improves overall efficiency.
originalProblem: Deep learning research involves a highly iterative, manual workflow of experiment design, execution, analysis, and refinement, leading to significant time consumption and delays.

Existing LLM-based agent systems and traditional AutoML frameworks do not support comprehensive, autonomous execution and iterative refinement of GPU-intensive deep learning experiments over extended periods, nor do they handle continuous monitoring effectively.
The prohibitive cost of continuous LLM API calls and unbounded context growth are major challenges for long-running autonomous deep learning agents.
results: Over 30 days of continuous operation across four projects, the agent completed over 500 autonomous experiment cycles, achieving a 52% improvement in the target metric for one project.

The framework demonstrated an average LLM cost of $0.08 per 24-hour cycle, representing a 10-20x cost reduction compared to conventional LLM polling agents due to zero-cost monitoring and other optimizations.
The two-tier constant-size memory system maintained its bounded size effectively, and safety mechanisms like mandatory dry-runs prevented 18% of potential full GPU training failures.
solution: A Deep Researcher Agent framework autonomously manages the entire research lifecycle, including hypothesis formation, code implementation, experiment execution, result analysis, and iterative refinement, operating 24/7 through a continuous THINK→EXECUTE→REFLECT loop.
Zero-Cost Monitoring is implemented to eliminate LLM API costs during the lengthy model training phase by performing lightweight OS-level checks for process liveness, GPU utilization, and log tails.

A Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory architecture maintains a fixed, small memory footprint (approximately 5,000 characters) to prevent unbounded context growth, while a Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker Architecture reduces token overhead for specialized tasks.
paper_summary.summary: The Deep Researcher Agent, developed by Xiangyue Zhang at The University of Tokyo, provides an autonomous framework for continuous, 24/7 deep learning experimentation, automating hypothesis generation, code execution, and result analysis. It achieves economic viability through a novel zero-cost monitoring approach and architectural innovations, demonstrating a 10-20x cost reduction and a 52% metric improvement in a target project over 30 days.

title: Deep Researcher Agent: An Autonomous Framework for 24/7 Deep Learning Experimentation with Zero-Cost Monitoring

abstract: The equivariant cohomology ring of a regular semisimple Hessenberg variety in type A is a free module over the equivariant cohomology ring of a point. When equipped with Tymoczko's dot action, it becomes a twisted representation of the symmetric group, and the character of this representation is given by the chromatic quasisymmetric function of an indifference graph. In this note, we use divided difference operators to decompose this representation as a direct sum of sub-representations in a way that categorifies the modular relation between chromatic quasisymmetric functions.
